WOMAN'S EXPONENT
		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JUNE 15, 1877.        No. 2


THE SILK WORM'S WILL
	BY MISS H. F. GOULD

On a plain rush hurdle a silk work lay,
When a proud young princess came that way,
The haughty child of a human king,
Threw a sidelong glance at the humble thing,
That took with a silent gratitude
From the mulberry leaf-her simple food-
And shrank with half scorn and half disgust,
Away from her sister, child of dust;
Declaring she never yet could see
Why a reptile form like that should be,
And that she was not made with nerves so form,
As to calmly stand by a “crawling worm!"
With mute forbearance the silk-worm took
The taunting words and the spurning look;
A stranger alike to self and pride,
She had no disquiet from aught beside,
But lived of a meekness and peace possessed,
Which these debar from the human breast.
She only wished, for the harsh abuse,
To find some way to become of  use
To the haughty daughter of lordly man,
And thus did she lay a noble plan,
To teach her wisdom and make it plain,
That the humble worm was not made in vain;
A plan so generous so deep and high
That to carry it out she must even die.
"No more," said she, "will I drink or eat!
I'll spin and weave me a winding sheet,
to wrap me up from the sun's clear light,
And to hide my form from that wounded sight.
In secret then, till my end draws nigh
I'll toil for her; and when I die,
I'll leave behind as a farewell boon,
To the proud young princess my full cocoon;
To be reeled and woven in shining lace,
And hung in a vail o’er her scornful face;
She will softly smile as she draws her breath,
Through the very threads that have caused my
	death:
When she finds, at length, she has nerve so firm,
As to wear the shroud of a "crawling worm."
May she bear in mind that she walks with pride,
In the winding sheet where the silk worm died.


A FEW REFLECTIONS.

			    (CONTINUED.)

 Beside blessing Abraham and the lineage
of this patriarchal tree, God also blessed
Sarai and said she should be called Sarah
and should bear a son, and become a mother
of nations, and kings of nations should be
of her posterity, Genesis xv chapt., 15, 16
verses.
	In the xxxii chapt. of Genesis, Jacob is
spoken of journeying with his wives, and 
the angels of God met him on his way, and
Jacob called them God's host.  And this
same Jacob was one of the main branches
of this polygamic tree.  In the same chap-
ter beginning at the 24th verse there is an
account given of Jacob wrestling with a
man, and prevailing; and Jacob knowing
this man was one of great power, would
not let him go until he should bless him;
and he obtained the blessing he desired,
and his name was called Israel, "for as a
prince hast thou power with God and with
man and hast prevailed."  And Jacob asked
his name, but he evaded answering the
question by asking another, "and Jacob
called the name of the place Peniel; for I
have seen God face to face and my life is
preserved," Genesis xxxii chapt., 30 verse.
We find again in the xxxv chapt., begin-
ning at the 9th verse, God appeared again
unto him and made the same promises to
him which He made unto his grandfather
Abraham; that he should be fruitful and
a nation and a company of nations should
come of his loins.  And the land which
God had given to his forefathers, Abraham
and Isaac should be given to him and his
seed after him.  And the Bible expressly
says he talked with God.  These testimo-
nies of the respect in which God held these
ancient patriarchs with their plural wives
abound throughout sacred history.
 Moses whom God called to lead the child-
ren of Israel out of bondage was also a man
who had plural wives.  We find in the xii
chapter of Numbers that the Lord instead
of condemming Moses for the practice,
made his power manifest unto Aaron and
Miriam who had spoken against it, and his
anger was kindled against them and Miriam
suffered severely and became leprous and
had to be shut out from the camp seven days
notwithstanding Moses cried unto the Lord
for her.  All who read this chapter may un-
derstand how dangerous it is to speak
against this practice which the Lord ap-
proves.  This is called in the Scriptures,
the sedition of Miriam and Aaron. This
instance scarcely leaves room for a doubt,
for no believer in the word of God as laid
down in the Bible would admit for a mo-
ment that God defends evil practices.
Here we have cited to you Abraham, Ja-
cob and Moses, which three witnesses an-
swer the end of the law to condemn or
justify. But this is a very interesting sub-
ject and there is so much depending upon
it that we will proceed still further with
testimony.
 In Kings xv chapter and 5th verse we 
read of David, a man after God's own heart
through whose loins a chosen seed should be
raised up, even Christ the Redeemer of Is-
rael, because David did that which was
right in the eyes of the Lord, and turned
not aside from anything that He command-
ed him all the days of his life, save only
in the matter of Uriah.  We find, also in
ii Samuel, xii chapter, 8th verse, the Lord
when reproving him for that sin said "And
I gave thee thy master's house and thy
master's wives into thy bosom, and gave
thee the house of Israel and the house of
Judah; and it that had been too little, I
would moreover have given unto thee such
and such things," showing that the Lord
considered many wives a great blessing
among other things which He had bestowed
upon him.  After the Lord had chastened
him and taken away by death the child be-
gotten in sin, He remembered the cove-
nant He had made with his father before
him saying that "inasmuch as ye do the
works of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, I the
Lord will greatly bless and multiply you."
 And thus must we confess that His say-
ings are true, when He says that His ways
are not as man's ways for instead of brand-
ing the woman with shame and casting her
off to become a victim of the vile and the
abandoned, and be dragged down to degra-
dation and death and although David had
taken her to wife after he had other wives,
yet the Lord blessed her together with
David her husband by giving them Solo-
mon whom the Lord loved; and whom He
chose above all the sons of David to sit
upon His Father's throne to reign over all
Israel, and build a Temple to the Lord.
Solomon also had many wives and we do
not find that he was condemned for any-
thing, only for marrying women of other
nations which the Lord had commanded
them they should not do.  I Kings xi chap-
ter 11th and 12th verses; he did not keep
the statutes and judgments as did David
his father, plainly showing that he was not
condemned for plurality of wives.


NOTES AND NEWS.
			     ----
 THE  NEW England Woman Suffrage As-
sociation held their ninth annual meeting,
commencing Monday evening May 28, at
Tremont Temple, Boston, Mass.  Mrs. Ju
lia Ward Howe the president of the Asso-
ciation being absent, Mr. Henry B. Black-
well called the meeting to order and laid the
business of the Convention before the as-
semblage.  Mr. I. T. Fields in the open-
ing appropriately, speech alluded to Sidney
Smith as being one of the earliest and
strongest champions of women’s rights.
Mrs. Lucy Stone in her remarks also allud-
ed to Sidney Smith, to whose disinterested
labors women all over the world were in-
debted.  She announced that Mrs. Mary A.
Livermore and Wendell Phillips would be
among the speakers at the convention on
Tuesday evening.  The business meeting
began in the Meionaon Tuesday morning,
with an audience of about five hundred.
The first business was the report of the
Chairman of the Executive Committee.
Mrs. Lucy Stowe read the report, which
stated in substance that the treasury had
been replenished through means raised by
a Festival and contributions so as to accom-
plish the work of the year without a debt.
 Many excellent speeches were made, and
reports from different societies brought for-
ward, appeals from various places to the As-
sociation in convention assembled, etc.  We
have not space to publish, or scarcely touch
on these speeches from distinguished people
of the world engaged in this public work,
but will give one paragraph from the speech
of Mrs. Mary A. Livermore concerning
Utah, which will show how falsely this
people are placed before the public, and
will call forth an article or two upon this
subject in our next issue.
 "In Utah women vote.  But both men
and women are ticketed, and receive from
the authorities a ballot corresponding with
the number they bear.  It is fair to say
that the women do not want to change them.
If men want women who always do exactly
what the men tell them, you will find them
in Utah.  These women command my sym-
pathy and respect.  They obey their reli-
gious convictions of duty.  The graveyards
of Utah are filled with women who have
died broken-hearted in obedience to their 
consciences; women who have given up
their husbands to other women because
they thought it to be their duty.”


Rebecca Andersen, of Seneca Falls N.
Y., is the oldest old maid in the world.
She is one hundred and twelve.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT


[Column 1]
	R. S. REPORTS.

	The General Retrenchment meeting in the
Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms, May 26
was very full, and one of very great inter-
est.  The president of the meeting and those
who addressed the assembly, dwelt mostly
upon the necessity of home-industry.
Counselor E. R. Snow laid before the sis-
ters the necessity of providing labor here
at home for the thousands who emigrate to
Zion every year; that they may have means
to obtain a livelihood; that this portion of
the work was as necessary as spiritual work,
or the work of building Temples, for if the
people did not become self-sustaining they
would not have the privilege of remaining
here to minister in those temples and receive
the ordinances and blessings pertaining
thereto; that while the gathering was still
being preached, it was essential that those
who gather here should be furnished with
suitable employment, that this had been
preached to the saints from the commence-
ment of settling these valleys.  It was nec-
essary now to do, and do with and for a
purpose, to take measures that would be
carried out by the people, everything was
being so arranged so as to make these
things possible, and the sisters could lend
efficient aid in these matters; she hoped
and desired to see them step forward and
show that they were really for Zion and
her interests.  Counselor S. M. Kimball
spoke on the same subject and referred to
the tannery the brethren were now trying
to establish. said she hoped every sister
who could raise five dollars would take a
share in it; for her own part she thought
this people did wrong in sending their
hides out of the territory to be worked up
when there was every facility for making
leather here in Utah, and it would be one
source of labor and remuneration to men
who had families to provide for; made ref-
erence to instances where men with fami-
lies were unemployed and needed work to
do to procure the necessaries of life.  Oth-
ers of the sisters spoke energetically on
this subject.  Many good things were said cal-
culated to give lasting impressions upon the
matter. The subject of straw manufacture,
was brought before themeeting and the time
was mostly spent in speaking upon those
things that have a bearing upon the every-
day affairs of life, and the duties and ob-
ligations of all who desire to see Zion free
and independent, and not bargained away
for a few paltry, flimsy, showy adornments
"that perish with the using," and are a part
and parcel of Satan's programme to bring
sorrow upon the people of God, and turn
them from the pathway of obedience.

EDITORS EXPONENT:
	Thinking you might like to hear what
we are doing in Hyde Park, we determin-
ed to report ourselves.  Our society is pro-
gressing, not rapidly, but as fast as possible;
we have nearly seventy bushels of wheat
securely stored and intend adding to it.
We have given our mite to assist with the
temple and factory.  We intend trying to
raise our own silk, and make our own wear-
ing apparel as much as possible.  As an as-
sociation we enjoy the spirit of the Lord
in our midst, which prompts us to try to
overcome our weaknesses and help in all
good works.  We have a Retrenchment As-
sociation here, presided over by Mrs. Sar-
ah Daines, it is in quite a prosperous con-
dition.  The EXPONENT is always joyfully
received and much information is gained by
perusing its columns.  We have had a great

[Column 2]
deal of sickness and many deaths; there
are few families but what have had to part
with one or more of their little ones.  We
acknowledge the hand of God in all things
and desire to serve Him and ever have His
spirit to guide and strengthen us.  If Miss
E. R. Snow, or Mrs. Z. D. Young, or any
of the sisters who take delight in encour-
aging each other, should be called by duty
or pleasure to Cache Co., we should be
most happy to have them pay us a visit.
That the women of our most holy faith
may have power to do the good they de-
sire, to exalt themselves above the follies
of mankind, is the prayer of your sisters,
	MARY L. WOOLF,
	President.
	MARY E. GRIFFETH,
	Secretary.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

FARMINGTON, DAVIS COUNTY,
			May 6th, 1877.

EDITORS EXPONENT:

	I have often felt impressed with a great
desire to write my feelings and the thoughts
that pass through my mind while medita-
ting on the work of God, and as I am a 
great thinker I have been led of late to con-
template the glorious and most wonderful
workings of the Almighty, and it causes
my heart to rejoice that I live in this day
and hear the servants of God talk in plain-
ness of the things which pertain to our
eternal salvation and exaltation in the
kingdom of God; they speak with power,
and it thrills my soul with joy while listen-
ing to their teachings, and I feel willing to
put away all my selfishness and think, Oh!
that I could live by every word that pro-
ceedeth from the mouth of God through
His servants.
 The cares and duties of a large family
prevent me from filling responsible posi-
tions as some of my sisters are so faithfully
doing, but I can fill a place which calls my
attention daily and hourly and which I can-
not neglect without feeling remorse of con-
science. It is our binding duty as mothers
in Israel to watch over our little ones to
keep them as much as we can out of dan-
ger. Those precious gifts which God has
given me, words cannot express the anx-
iety I feel regarding their welfare, especial-
ly those who are old enough to be taught
those principles whicn will help them to
fill honorable places in society, and become 
useful wives and good mothers. Our child-
ren are our hope, and on us depend their
future happiness in life. Keep them from
the company of those who would lead them
astray; it requires patience and a prayerful
heart, and it is no easy task to keep our
children in the paths of virute; we must
pray for strength from on high to enable
us to fill that high and holy calling, and
great will be our joy if we are permitted to
have those jewels in a heavenly sphere,
throughout the endless ages of eternity;
then will it not reward us for all our watch-
care and trouble, our toil and sorrow, if we
can accomplish this, by the help of God?
although we may work with fear and trem-
bling, if we save our sons and daughters
we will have accomplished something great.
Home and children are a blessing to wo-
man, and it should be her highest ambition
to set a good example before them especial-
ly as it falls more on the mother to train
them when the father is absent from the
family circle. The mother should call her
children together and bow in humble pray-
er for the light of the holy spirit to lead
them in the path of duty, this will give
that mother strength that she may endure
faithful while performing the most arduous
tasks which devolve upon her; she who is
sometimes so tired, and weary and would
be glad of an opportunity to retire to some
place of solitude for rest from the noise
and bustle which seems almost distracting
and unendurable at times.
						S. L. C.

 How little woman's labor is appreciated
by the husbands and fathers, they shrink
from some of their most important duties,
and leave too great a responsibility upon the
mother, and apparently can enjoy ease and
comfort elsewhere, while their sons are
breaking the Sabbath day, or doing that
which grieves a mother to the hearts core.
Oh fathers in Israel, when will you see to
this most essential of duties? the Lord re-
quires this at your hands as well as at the
mother's and great will be your condemna-
tion in a day to come if you neglect it. I
beg pardon if I have used too much free-
dom of sentiment, but "out of the abund-
ance of the heart the mouth speaketh."
We must be faithful to our calling, and
when we have finished our work and our
bodies are worn out and laid low in the
dust, our names will be held in sweet re-
membrance by those who are dear to us,
although they seem not to appreciate us
while we are with them.
 I do not feel capable of teaching those
who are much more advanced in knowledge
than myself; but you, with me my sisters,
can teach our daughters to keep good com-
pany, the company of good men and wo-
men, those superior to themselves in good-
ness. The education of household duties is
just as essential for our girls, as knowledge
of books; we are in our houses more than
half of our existence and it is the house-
hold surroundings which affect most large-
ly the happiness or misery of life. If our
daughters learn the art of making home a
desirable place for their husbands and child-
ren, if they acquire the way of governing
quietly, if they have to tact to observe or-
der, and practice cleanliness, for cleanliness
is next to godliness, then they know how
to make a little heaven at home; how to re-
turn the affection of their children. To
have home smiling with comfort and cheer-
fulness, is a happy solace to a weary moth-
er. 
	God has blessed me exceedingly; may
His blessing rest upon all, is the prayer of
your sister in the Gospel.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Your Splinter from "The Watchman"
 is only partially correct. The "Regent" or
"Pitt Diamond" is acknowledged to be the
best proportioned in the world, is exceed-
ingly brilliant, weight 136 carats, required
two years to cut it, and is valued at $1,000,-
000. The most valuable, it is claimed is
the "Grand Mogul" which weighs 7801/2 car-
ats in the rough, but is reduced to 279 and
nine sixteenths by the process of cutting;
nevertheless it is valued at the modest sum
of $2,400,000.


Nothing on earth is so beautiful as the
household in which love ever smiles, and
religion walks a counselor and a friend.
No storms can make it tremble, for it has
a heavenly anchor. The home circle sur-
rounded by such influences, has the [-]nte-
past of the joys of a heavenly home.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


TO SALT LAKE CITY.

O City of the wilderness,
     Made beauteous by untiring toll;
Industry have thee lovliness,
     And fertilized they barren soil.
When sunshine gilds the western skies,
     Enwreathing thee in dazzling green,
Greeting the travelers' weary eyes
    Thou seem'st the desert's queen.

Thou'st made thy throne the eternal hills,
    Whose strength is freedom's guarantee,
Whose treasured snows, the mountain rills,
    And canon torrents bear to thee.
When "Sol" in radiance on us beams,
    And freshness given to all things here,
What robes of green 'mid sparkling streams
    Thy glorious summer's wear.

Made great by precious trophies won, 
    Of mind and skill, from many lands
Where want and sin their courses run
    And error gilds her strengthened bands.
Won in the power of truth divine--
    Here is the truth of God adored,
Not dragged in chains in grief to pine,
    Not gathered by the sword.

Shall slavery ever drag thee down?
    Not while they mountains kiss the sky,
While round thee craggy strong holds frown
    True liberty shall never die,
Nor avarice plant unholy bowers
    To desecrate the sacred soil.
Want's rude despoiler raze they towers
    To mock the sweat of honest toil,

Already worth's engraved thy name,
    Undimmed with blood, on history's page;
Thy genius and they new-born fame
    Rebuke the spirit of the age-
Wrong rampant, virtue in the shade, 
    Wealth lending might power to ill,
Religion mean and servile made, 
    To speak wealth's sovereign will. 

Dear sought out refuge of the meek, 
    Be thou the home of liberty!
With truth and wisdom may we week
    To light thy future destiny.
Science, industry, faith and lgiht
    Have come to worship from afar,
To hail thy birth and heaven-born right,
    And be thy guiding star.
				R. SMITH.
15th Ward, Salt Lake City


PATRIARCHAL MARRIAGE.
	The subject of patriarchal or plural mar-
raige is generally interesting to every one
especially to the outside world. Almost
the first question asked our missionaries by
the Gentiles, is "how many wives have
you got?" when they become aware they are
Latter-day Saints and from Utah.  An im-
mense interest is created when plural mar-
raige is mentioned. There are very few
that understand the principle of patriarch-
al marriage in its true light. The beauty
and consistency of it is only seen by those
that possess the spirit of the Lord; which
spirit enables us to see and understand 
things pertaining to life everlasting, and it
will comfort and cheer us in all the trials
which we are called to pass through. This
is a principle through which Satan tries us
with all his might; he shows us (that is if
we allow him to) that mole hills are about
the size of mountains, and little things,
which at other times we would pass by un-
noticed; he makes to appear very dreadful 
to bear. Blessed are the women who will
sustain this principle in the face of all oppo-
sition: Such indeed are mothers in Israel,
and true daughters of the living God.
When men and women take upon them-
selves these holy covenants, they should be
so sure that they are in the path of duty,
that all  busybodies there are cannot in-
fluence them or cause them one dark hour.
Sisters when a person comes to you and
speaks against this holy principle and tries
to discourage you in fulfilling the law given,
do not yield to the influence, but endeavor


to show to them the principle in its purity,
and how perfect should be the practice of
it. A part of woman's mission is to com-
fort and cheer those around her, especially
those of her own household; the wife
should comprehend the greater glory she
is attaining to through every wife added
to the family. The Lord fits the back for
the burdens and He blesses those who obey
Him in this principle in ways that are won-
derful; and besides if we are faithful and
keep the commandments of the Lord we
lay on this earth the foundation of eternal 
progression.
 Our religion is a practical religion, we
carry it out in our every day lives, and by
doing, our duty and living up to our privi-
liges and blessings and keeping our eye on
the mark, we help to roll on the kingdom
of God, and fulfill the mission we were sent
here to do. We should so live that when
our children grow up they may feel that
Plural Marriage is far superior to the one
wife system, and that they may not have
the prejudices to overcome that we have
had through tradition, and that our child-
ren may be as reconciled to it as the child-
ren of Jacob were. 
 Then sisters let us sustain this principle
with our whole heart and soul, for there are
noble spirits anxiously waiting to take tab-
ernacles here, and if the women in the
kingdom of God don’t fulfill the first great
law given to them, then others must be
raised up who will.
 Asking the Lord to bless all the daugh-
ters of Zion, and that all may attain to Ce-
lestial Glory who are earnestly striving for
it, is my sincere prayer.
					LIZZIE LEAKER.


WOMAN'S VOICE

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Our little settlement is trying to progress,
We are doing the best we can, although we
have had a great deal of sickness the past
winter; yet we feel to acknowledge the
hand of God in it for we are told "whomso-
ever the Lord loveth He chasteneth, and
that He is displeased with none save it be
those that will not acknowledge His hand
in all things." In the midst of all this
sickness we have seen the power of God
made manifest many times, and I know
that if we are faithful and prayerful before
God we can do much good; and whatever
we set our minds to do the Lord will bless,
whether it is to lay up grain, or to aid in
building temples; these are things that
every one is interested in or should be; and
let us as sisters try to live our religion at
home, if we can do this it will be much to
our credit; and let us be ever ready to re-
spond to the calls of those that are placed
over us: may the Lord help us to do so is the
wish of you sister in the Gospel,
						LYDIA RICH.
	Morgan City, May 20, 1877.

	Sisters I have reflected much of late up-
on the position we occupy as helpmeets to
the brethren in the cause of Zion, and have
often thought I would like to cast my mite
among those who write for our Woman's
paper; their words of comfort and encour-
agement are of great worth to me, and I
trust to all those who are interested in the
Gospel. The condition of the women
here in Utah, is something much talked
of now, and pondered upon by those
who are seriously inclined. They are de-
sirous to know something of these degrad-


ed women who are considered a disgrace to
the great American Republic. We are
willing that they should know concerning
our history, our practical lives. We are
free to do right, we are free to do wrong.
We know what is the will of our heavenly
Father concerning us, and how much better
for us to do it, than to turn from it to the
vanities and abominations of the world.
The will of the Lord to us is to enter into
the covenants of patriarchal marriage and
raise up pure seed unto Him, who will not
be corrupted with the evils that are prac-
ticed in this nineteenth century, by the so-
called ladies and gentlemen of the age.
But the cry is “put these Mormons to death,
they are not fit to live among such as we,”
—Yes you that are destroying your off-
spring and that have become so degenerat-
ed as not to be willing to fulfill the first
commandment "to multiply and replenish
the earth." You that are reveling in lux-
ury and fashion, and draining to the dregs
the cup of iniquity, some under the cloak
of religion, others more openly, should
know that the light of the Everlasting Gos-
pel has come into the world, and all have a
chance for repentance, and all will be re-
warded according to their works.
	Praying that the Lord will bless the sis-
ters in the great work of redemption in
which we are engaged for the living and
for the dead, and desiring to be a co-worker
in this great and glorious cause, I will now
close.
						S. C. D.


THE CAVALCADE. 

	Merrily sounded the clattering hoofs as
the gay riders swept past on their spirited
steeds, their cheeks as roseate as the bright,
June morning. Hastily joining them, I
was soon drinking in the inspiration of the
fragrant air, which seemed heavily laden
with rich spices and perfumes. After
a short canter over the hills, one of the
cool, shady canons east of our city, was
reached. It is needless to describe the
scenery, all such delightful places, and glor-
ious mornings have a similarity. Birds,
bloom and fragrance were everywhere
around, and fresh, mountain breezes fan-
ning the cheeks, caused the pulses to quick-
en and the heart to glow with newly invig-
orated life and hope. The day was spent
among nature's wildest haunts, culling the
different varieties of regal and modest flow-
ers, and weaving them into garlands of the
richest dyes. Sol sunk to rest, as the gay
cavalcade wended their way towards the
city again, their forms wreathed and dec-
orated in the beautiful bands of bloom from
the wildwoods and valleys. The world
seemed very bright to them, and the
music of their youthful voices floated forth
on the evening air as they neared the
“dearest spot on earth," HOME
 Let those who are over-burdened with
care, forget for a time the busy, bustling
world, and seek out some of nature's beau-
tiful bowers, and there surrounded by the
mighty works of the Great Omnipotent, in-
hale the pure breath from the clear, cool
heavens; it will be as balm to the weary
soul, and impart strength and fortitude, to
again take up the cross of life. 
						 ZOE.


GENUINE neighborly love knows no dis-
tinction of persons. It is like the sun,
which does not ask on what it shall shine
or what it shall warm, but shines and warms
by the very laws of its own being.

	SALT LAKE CITY, JUNE 15, 1877.


[Column 1]

HOME MANUFACTURE, AND
STORING GRAIN.

	There are many practical subjects of vital
interest now to the women of this church and
kingdom which ought to be taken into consid-
eration, and acted upon, as well as talked
about or written upon. This people are called
upon to be self-sustaining; to produce all that
is possible from the soil and from the elements,
and to manufacture all articles practicable at
home, and invent improvements in all the de-
partments of industry, which will be labor sav-
ing or time saving, and encourage all attempts
made in that direction, that will help to make
us as a people in reality what we have been so
long entreated to be, independent of Babylon;
that when she shall fall, as she inevitably
must, the Latter-day Saints may not be left
destitute of necessary comforts and blessings,
because they were not willing to hearken un-
to the voice of the Lord. But we cannot be-
lieve this will be the case. Surely the faith
the saints profess is real, and will bear the test
of endurance, though great exertions must be
made, and much self-denial practiced. After
having succeeded in establishing civilization
in a desolate country, enduring all the trials
and hardships consequent upon such an enter-
prise, this people of all people upon the earth
should be the last to go back to those who
thrust them out from their midst to perish of
hunger or cold, or in any other manner, es-
teeming them too vile to occupy cultivated
lands and homes. This people owe no grati-
tude to the world outside, that they should la-
bor and toil and accumulate means to support
Babylon. What obligations are the saints un-
der to their enemies? None save such as all
creatures owe to each other to do good for evil.
If people could only believe it, only compre-
hend it in its fullest and complete sense, they
are not even doing the good to the outside
world which they would be doing were they
to combine together and determine to be self-
sustaining. If this people will make the one con-
centrated effort they are now called upon to 
make, and not think they will do it at some
future time, but now, they will not become
slaves to Babylon and her wares, but will be
well prepared to administer to her in the day
of adversity, when "the hour of her travail"
comes. What is there now in this territory if
the great crisis which the saints well know is
impending were to come suddenly? Nothing,
except it may be in one or two parts of the
territory where home-industries have been
practically tried for a short time, but for the
majority, poverty and wretchedness, and mis-
ery. Shall we all be able to realize these great
temporal truths?
	There is every inducement offered now to

[Column 2]

raise silk-worms. It is positively demon-
strated that the soil, the climate and all
the facilities are indigenous here in Utah
to the production of silk. There has
been some tangible materials made here
from the elements in the silk line of
goods. Some of the ladies have silk dresses
manufactured from the elements of Utah; and
there are for sale at the Woman's Commission
House, scarfs, pocket-handkerchiefs, sewing
silk, floss and laces of superior beauty and
quality, all home work and home material.
Miss E. R. Snow has a bonnet made of the silk-
dress material which looks very elegant, trim-
med with the home-made black silk lace and
artificial flowers. There will soon be a variety
of homemade ribbons.
	Another subject of the utmost interest to the
women of this territory, is the saving and stor-
ing of grain. We hope none of the good sisters
have forgotten the counsel given to them by
President Young last fall on this subject. We
do not wish to relinquish OUR efforts in any
sense in this direction in the least. We feel
that it is something the sisters have been called
to do by revelation, and let each one of us
be wise and use all the energy we possess to 
save every kernel of wheat raised this season,
and if it is really necessary that men should
sell or dispose of their grain for other things;
let the Relief Societies in the different settle-
ments be prepared to buy as much as possible,
and store it away carefully using every precau-
tion to preserve it from damp and insects of 
all kinds. We hope the sisters will also be
wise in preparing suitable places for storage,
now before the harvest comes on; take into
consideration all due preparation, and do not
be afraid you will not get the grain to store for
you will. This is not a trifling matter nor a
thing of little consequence, but a work that
demands some of the best thoughts and exec-
utive ability that woman has ever been called
upon to put into action. Many of the sisters
and children have told us they intend glean-
ing: no doubt the wheat fields will be filled
with gleaners this season. If the faith of the
sisters is good, there will be no danger of their
not having large quantities of grain to store,
and remember it is food for you and your
children in the future when war, desola-
tion and famine shall come, and the saints
know it will come. Remember this is salva-
tion both in a temporal and spiritual sense,
and it is worthy the sacrifice of fine clothes
and all superfluities. May the Lord aid and
bless the sisters throughout the territory in
this noble enterprise.


HOME AFFAIRS.
	WE RETURN sincere thanks to Mrs. Emma
Wagstaff for two very elegant bouquets of choice
and beautiful flowers, received recently; these
are kind remembrnaces, most particularly wel-
come to those sitting all day at the desk, with-
out the opportunity of leisure to go and inhale
their refreshing fragrance in the gardens, or
wildwoods.
	DIED AT Huntsville, Weber Co., June 6th,
1877, of heart disease, Mary Jane Dilworth,
wife of Bishop F. A. Hammond and President
of the Relief Society in that place.  She went
with her husband as a missionary to the Sand-
wich Islands in 1850, where she taught the
native children during six years and three
months and was much beloved by them, so
much that they gave her the title of mother.
Her  name will ever be remembered there with
honor and gratitude.  Her death has cast a 
gloom over the whole community where she
resided.


CENTREVILLE, DAVIS Co., June 6th.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Our Society has lost one of our aged mem-
bers, Margaret Bleasdell, who departed this life
May 26, in her eightieth year; she left a hus-
band, aged eighty-two years, and several chil-
dren (some of whom are in the States). She
had over fifty grandchildren and a number of
great-grandchildren. She always took great
interest in every movement that was for the
advancement of the kingdom of God; it was
her delight to converse on the principles of the
gospel. She was a subscriber of the EXPONENT
for many years. She died as she had lived a
true Latter-day Saint.—COM.

	THIS CITY in its growth and improvement is
fast developing into a similarity with older cit-
ies and towns in its variety of public entertain-
ments. Professional lecturers, both men and
women of the different varieties of style and
sentiments, on all popular and unpopular sub-
jects, contrive to give Salt Lake the benefit of
their wonderful knowledge "for value re-
ceived." Traveling Dramatic and Minstrel
Troupes make it a part of their programme to
perform once or more in Salt Lake City, and
almost every year this city is visited once or
twice by a circus and caravan. Thus Salt Lake
is quite up to the modern standard in this sort
of amusement. This season there has already
been quite a number of these performances of
rather a superior quality. One of the most
famous American orators, Theodore Tilton,
gave his celebrated lecture "The Problem of
Life," to an attentive and appreciative audi-
ence in the Theatre recently. He declared
many great but simple truths, and his lecture
contained much that, if practised, would very
materially benefit the condition of mankind.
We failed to see any solution of the problem of
life in his remarks, except that it is always
wisest and best to do right in preference to
wrong, and "honesty is the best policy."

	IT SEEMS that there are sometimes people
who visit Utah and tell the truth, at least a
part of it. It is really refreshing to have once
in a while a few words spoken in our favor by
those who are not of us. L. B. Fyfield, of
Kearney, Neb., who recently visited Salt Lake
with the party of editors from Nebraska, a
man interested and acquainted with education-
al matters, says in the "Daily Press" Kearney,
Nebraska:
	"The editor of this column has had the pleas-
ure of visiting the Deserrt University and oth-
er schools in Salt Lake City. His full report
upon this and other subjects, will be embraced
in a lecture. But it should be said here that
Salt Lake City is not wanting in the substan-
tials of English education. It should also be
said, that there seems to be misapprehension
of the educational opinions of Brigham Young.
He is not opposed to education. He is opposed
to a system that is confined to books. He
would have every child taught how to get a
living by "work." He would educate the
hand as well as the head. The teaching and
the studying in Salt Lake City are far ahead of
the buildings occupied by the schools. In the
states it is often the other way. Brigham
Young may be no saint. Nevertheless, there
are the schools in Salt Lake City, Mormon
schools and excellent."
	D. S. A. MEETING.—The Deseret Silk Asso-
ciation met in the Fireman's Hall, Friday
June 1, pursuant to adjournment, Mrs. Horne
presiding.
	Mrs. Horne said it was a benefit to us to


meet together from time to time to be instruct-
ed how to proceed in this branch of business to
make it a success, and what was most needed
now was machinery to work up our silk into
articles of wearing apparel. She was in favor
of working our silk at home and not sending
it east, but to give employment to those in
our own community, and by this means keep
labor and capital at home. She was in favor
of each ward having a cocoonery to give em-
ployment to those who might not be able to
work at more laborious work, and by this mean
it would enable some to be earning a livlihood
where otherwise they would be idle. The
beauty of our adornment ought to be the
workmanship of our own hands.
	Mr. Paul A. Schettler endorsed the remarks
of the previous speaker. If the people would
only take hold of this business and raise the
cocoons, there was nothing to hinder us from
making it a success. Our silk was as good as
any in the world. He had received a letter
from a gentleman in the east to say that he
would procure the necessary machinery for our
present use for six hundred and fifty dollars.
Said that one pound of silk would make from
fourteen to fifteen yards of dress silk. He was
in favor of getting the best machinery. By
present appearances there would be four times
the amount of silk raised this year than any
year before. He advised those who could not
raise cocooneries to fit up a room and be gain-
ing some experience.
	The meeting was then addressed by Mr. H.
F. Slagowski, of St. George, Mrs. Robinson, of
Farmington, and others, after which a vote of
thanks was tendered Mrs. Robinson for having
made the first piece of silk dress goods in
Utah.
	Meeting was adjourned until the first Friday
in July, at 3 p. m., at this place.
	R. SIMPSON, Sec'y.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

ITEMS FROM THE DIARY OF 
JOSEPH SMITH.

TO THE RELIEF SOCIETY.

[CONTINUED]

	Nauvoo, April 28th,1842.—He then commen-
ced to read the xiii chapter of Cor. "Though
I speak with the tongues of men and angels, and
have not charity, I am become as sounding
brass,or a tinkling symbal; and said, don't be
limited in your views in regard to your neigh-
bor's virtues, but beware of self-righteousness,
and be limited in the estimate of your own vir-
tues, and not consider yourselves more righteous
than others; you must enlarge your souls to
wards each other if you would do like Jesus,
and carry your fellow-creatures to Abram's
bosom. He said he had manifested long-suf-
fering, forbearance and patience toward the
church, and also to his enemies, and we must
bear with each other's failings, as an indulgent
parent bears with the foibles of his children.
	President Smith then read the 2nd verse.
"Though I have the gift of prophecy, and un-
derstand all mysteries and all knowledge; and
though I have all faith, so that I could remove
mountains, and have not charity, I am noth-
ing." He then said, though a man should be-
come mighty, do great things, overturn moun-
tains, perform mighty works, and should then
turn from his high position and do evil, to eat
and drink with the drunken, all his former
deeds will not save him, but he would go to
destruction! As you increase in innocence and
virtue, as you increase in goodness, let your
hearts expand, let them be enlarged towards

[Column 2]

others; you must be long suffering, and bear
with the faults and errors of mankind.
	How precious are the souls of men! The fe-
male part of the community are apt to be con-
tracted in their views. You must not be con-
tracted, but you must be liberal in your feelings.
Let this Society teach women how to behave
towards their husbands, to treat them with
mildness and affection. When a man is borne
down with trouble, when he is perplexed with
care and difficulty, if he can meet a smile in-
stead of an argument or a murmur—if he can
meet with mildness, it will calm down his
soul and soothe his feelings; when the mind is
going to despair, it needs a solace of affection
and kindness.
	You will receive instruction through the or-
er of the Priesthood which God has established,
through the medium of those appointed to
lead, guide, and direct the affairs of the church
in this last dispensation; and I now turn the
key in your behalf in the name of the Lord,
and this Society shall rejoice, and knowledge
and intelligence shall flow down from this
time henceforth; this is the beginning of bet-
ter days to the poor and needy, who shall be
made to rejoice and pour forth blessings on
your heads.
	When you go home, never give a cross or
unkind word to your husbands, but let kind-
ness, charity and love crown your works hence-
forward; don't envy the finery and fleeting
show of sinners, for they are in a miserable
situation, but as far as you can, have mercy
on them, for in a short time God will destroy
them, if they will not repent and turn unto
Him.
	Let your labors be mostly confined to those
around you, in the circle of your own acquaint-
ance, as far as knowledge is concerned it may
extend to all the world; but your administra-
tions should be confined to the circle of your
immediate acquaintance, and more especially
to the members of the Relief Society. Those
ordained to preside over and lead you, are au-
thorized to appoint the different officers, as the
circumstances shall require.
	If any have a matter to reveal, let it be in
your own tongue, do not indulge too much in
the exercise of the gift of tongues, or the devil
will take advantage of the innocent and un-
wary. You may speak in tongnes for your
own comfort, but I lay it down for a rule, that
if anything is taught by the gift of tongues, it
is not to be received for doctrine.
	President Smith then gave instruction re-
specting the propriety of females administer-
ing to the sick by the prayer of faith, the lay-
ing on of hands or the anointing with oil;
said it was according to revelation that the
sick shoold be nursed with herbs and with
mild food, and not by the hand of an enemy.
Who are better qualified to administer than
our faithful and zealous sisters, whose hearts
are full of faith, tenderness, sympathy and
compassion? No one. Said he was never
placed in similar circumstances before, and
never had given the same instruction; and
closed his instructions by expressing his heart-
felt satisfaction in improving this opportunity.
	The spirit of the Lord was poured out in a
very powerful manner, never to be forgotten
by those present on this interesting occasion.


[Column 1]
	EXTRACTS FROM LETTERS FROM
		HAWAIIAN ISLANDS.

			BY JANE E. MOLEN.

	Our home is situated on a sloping hill.
Just above us on the south west is the re-
mains of a once active crater, which is now
the grave-yard of Laie. South is a beauti-
ful plain with sugar-cane under cultivation.
South east, east, and north east of us, roar-

[Column 2]
ing continually, is the great Pacific Ocean.
North and north west are mountains and
hills covered with green shrubbery, always
green, summer and winter. It is the most
magnificent scenery I ever beheld. The
most beautiful shapes and forms of trees
growing out of rocks; water gushing out of
the top of a mountain so high and perpen-
dicular that you can scarcely believe your
own sight. We are on a knoll with a pick-
et fence around the houses of Elders Wool-
ley, Lambert, Laie store and Plantation
House. The natives build their houses of
grass in the shape of a nice hay stack.

	I would be pleased to write a description
of the Sandwich Islands, had I seen more of
them, and felt myself competent; but I
think it would take a person with excellent
language to portray the beauties of this is-
land scenery; how I would like to be able
to describe it.
	We had the pleasure of a short, but very
pleasant visit from the Queen on the 28th
of February. She with her suite comprising
eighteen or twenty, mounted on fine horses
came dashing up to the door, which was
the first to give us warning of their in-
tended visit, there being some mistake in
the sending of the notice. Her business
was to organize Relief Societies. The peo-
ple were called together and she made a
speech, showing the necessity of such or-
ganizations, that the wants of the sick and
destitute might be administered to, and
thereby preserve the lives of many of the
nation. After the close of the meeting she
took supper with us, and continued her
tour around the Island. Queen Kapiolani
is a fine looking woman, pleasant and agree-
able in her manner, a little above medium
size, rather fleshy, very qnick and active,
being able to mount her steed without as-
sistance.
	The Elders here are well and in good
spirits. The mission is progressing slowly.
The term used by our Savior would well ap-
ply to this people "forgive seventy times
seven." Our annual conference here is
over. We have had an excellent time, all
our missionaries being here and all feeling
well. We look forward to our conference
with much pleasure as we can all meet and
enjoy the society of each other for a short
time. Our elders enjoy the spirit of their
mission. They have given good counsel to
the Saints here, and all seemed to enjoy it,
and want to try to do better in the future,
and live nearer to the Lord. In looking
over the congregation, I compared them
with our saints at home. I could see their
faces light up and fairly glow with the good
spirit as they listened to the counsels of
our Elders. The elders have been appoint-
ed their fields of labor as follows: Elders
R. G. Lambert, and H. S. Woolley to labor
here on the plantation in connection with
President Pack; Elders H. P. Richards and
J. S. Woodbnry on Hawaii; Elders S. M.
Molen and Jacob Gates on Maui, Molokai,
and Sanai; Elder James Keeler, Kanai and
Oahu.
	Her Majesty the Queen Kapiolani, at-
tended our Island conference, held a[-] Hono-
lulu on the first day of April-and appeared
to be much pleased with the preaching.
Elders Richards and Pack had an interview
with His Majesty the King, on the second,
and presented him with some Church Books
which Elder F. A. Mitchell sent him. He
received them very kindly and appeared
pleased with the books. He inquired after
his subjects in Utah, appeared anxious
about their welfare.

	LAIE, OAHU, April 17, 1877.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SUFFRAGE CONVENTION IN
NEW YORK.

 The friends of Woman Suffrage connect-
ed with the New York Society, met last
Thursday, May 24, in Masonic Hall. The
convention was called to order by Mrs. Isa-
bella Beecher Hooker. Mrs. Olympia
Browne made a prayer, and Mrs. Matilda
Joslyn Gage read letters of regret from
Miss Susan B. Anthony, Mrs. Elizabeth
Cady Stanton, and others. At two o'clock
a second session of the Association was held.
The attendance was larger than in the
morning. Mrs. Isabella Beecher Hooker
presented the report of the Committee on
Nominations, which was adopted and Mrs.
Clemence S. Lozier, M.D, was elected
President. A Vice-President was also chos-
en for each State and Territory, among the
persons selected being Lucretia Mott, Eliz-
abeth Cady Stanton, the Smith sisters of
Glastonbury, Conn., and Elizabeth Boynton
Harbert, of Illinois. Ernestine L. Rose
was elected Foreign Corresponding Secre-
tary, Mrs. Lillie Devereux Blake, Recording
Secretary, and Ellen Clarke Sargent, Treas-
urer. Mrs. Spencer then spoke on the work
of the Congressional Committee, declaring
that its power had never been so thorough-
ly felt as during the past year. She gave a 
minute and interesting account of her ex-
perience at the Cincinnatti Convention.
Rev. Olpmpia Brown then delivered an ad-
dress advocating an appeal to Congress for
the enfranchising of Woman, and adducing
numerous authorities in support of her de-
claration that Congress has the power to
grant the right and that Suffrage is insepar-
able from citizenship.
	Resolutions were adopted demanding the
ballot for women, with the exercise of citi-
zenship independent of sex, protesting
against the memorial from Utah to Congress
asking the disfranchisement of women in
that Territory, and asking Congress to pass
the Sixteenth Amendment, acknowledging
the right of women to vote. To the resolu-
tions were added the names of all those
Senators and Representatives who had vo-
ted for Woman Suffrage, and who are
thanked by the Association.
 Mrs. Matilda Joslyn Gage gave an ac-
count of the work of the Executive Com-
mittee. Having formally thanked Hon.
George F. Hoar for rising in his place in 
the Cincinnatti Convention to ask a hearing
for Mrs. Sara J. Spencer the convention
took a recess until evening.
 Mrs. Dr. Clemence S. Lozier presided at
the evening session, which was largely at-
tended.  She read an address in which she
claimed that Woman's Right to Suffrage
was natural and inalienable.
 Mrs. Lillie Devereux Blake alluded to a
speech of a leading Democrat in Congress
last winter, in which he declared that no 
people had a right to resort to war so long
as they were not deprived of the ballot.
The women of this county are entitled to
rebel, as they are deprived of the ballot-box 
and politically are slaves.  The old law that
excluded idiots, criminals and women does
not seem to prevail, for she had noticed by
several paragraphs in the newspapers, that
paupers had recently voted in that State.
The practice of pardoning criminals the
day before election was a notorious one, but
she had found that the only prison from
which no one was pardoned last year was
the woman's prison at Sing Sing.  Instan-
ces were cited to show the sort of represent-
tation which was obtained in men by wo-
men. The reduction of teachers' salaries in
this City was explained. They have, she
said, no votes and no political influence. If
they were possessed of the right of Suffrage
it is not to be supposed for one instant that
they would be treated in this way any more
than the policemen. She asked for an open
field and a fair fight in this contest.
 Mrs. Matilda Joslyn Gage said she had let-
ters from old workers in the cause, living
in various States. The decision of the Su-
preme Court of the United States in the
case of Virginia L. Minor, remanding wo-
men back to the States for their citizenship,
was a false ruling on the face of it. The
foreign-born voter who takes up his right
to vote in a State from the United States
when he takes up his naturalization papers.
The black men were made voters by the
United States,  and Jefferson Davis and Rob-
ert Toombs are deprived of the right to
vote by the United States.  The women of
the country will exercise their right of pe-
tition, which is secure to all citizens, and
will drop their petitions for the removal of
their disabilities like hail-stones into th e
next Congess. Mrs. Gage amused the au-
dience and provoked applause by reading
an ingenious form of petition which she
proposed to use, similar to that submitted
for relieving former rebels from their disa-
bilities.
 Mrs. Blake said that in presenting her
petition she had added the explanation that
she had never borne arms against the Uni-
ted States. She thought that explanation
ought to have given her better standing
than the rebels had, and it was her opinion
that women ought to have been enfranchis-
ed, before the rebels were.
 Mrs. Isabella Beecher Hooker spoke of
the progress made in the Woman Suffrage
movement in Connecticut, and alluded in-
cidentally to the fact election day had been
changed from spring to fall because New
York was too handy, and her citizens were
inclined to come over and meddle with 
Connecticut politics. Connecticut had re-
cently proposed that women who were pos-
sessed of $300 may vote at municipal elec-
tions, and a vote of forty-five members of
the Legislature was cast for it.  The conser-
vative Gov. Hubbard had spoken warmly
of the proposition to relieve women of their
disabilities, and his utterances had been
commended by newspapers, which said that
if the Woman’s Rights advocates had talk-
ed in this sensible way, something would
long ago have been accomplished. “Why,”
said the speaker, “we had published all and
more than the Governor said, in 50,000
tracts on the civil disabilities of women in
Connecticut,”' From what she had heard
to-day of Gov. Robinson;  New York State
would have to look to her laurels.  It seem-
ed to her that Governor Robinson had said
some very silly things, and that he is neith-
er the peer of Gov. Hubbard in intellect or
moral perception. “You will have to choose
another, or he must do better.  Silly is the
least objectionable term that can be applied
to your Governor."
	Mr. Lee, formerly of Wyoming, said that
the enfranchisement of women in that Ter-
ritory had proved a distinguished success.
The presence of women in official life had
changed the community from a lawless
mob to a law-abiding community, and he
had not discovered that any woman had
been unsexed by reason of her acquisition
of the privilege of voting.
 Dr. Lozier announced that the Conven-
tion was adjourned to meet in Washington
in January next.--"Woman Journal.".


IT is ignorance, and not knowledge, that
rejects instruction; it is weakness, and not
strength, that refuses co-operation.


A PROTEST.

	Sister Mabel:—I hope you will excuse
me if I appear blunt, and perhaps severe,
in my manner of expressing opinions about
your "Wishes and Suggestions," which ap-
peared in the EXPONENT of May 15th. It
is my nature to be plain spoken, and I
write as I speak.
	I consider the article referred to, the most
objectionable of anything that has ever
been published in our Woman's paper. It
seems contrary to the spirit of our religion
so uncalled for, uncharitable and unjust.
	Speaking of the honey bee as being "dis-
honest" for merely following the bent of its
nature, in sucking sweets from your or-
chard and garden flowers. And as "impo-
lite," for coming through an open window
to take honey from your table. In the first
place, it is nonsense to think that the bees
harm the fruit. A curse and a blight has
come upon our fruit because in former years
we have let the surplus waste, instead of
dividing it liberally with our poorer breth-
ren and sisters. The Lord will not suffer
His people to waste the blessings which
He gives them, without being punished
for so doing; and thus their blessings are
cursed to them. In the second place, there
is no need for the bees to trouble you by
coming through the open windows. A
piece of musquito bar may easily be
stretched across the aperture, and all such
trouble will be obviated. It appears to me
absurd to think of banishing our honey
bees from the city. Why, all through the
States they are kept in and about people's
homes.
	You speak of children who admire and
ask for flowers, as "little beggers," which I
think is very ungenerous. I should take
much pleasure myself, in having the little
things toddle in to ask me for a rose; and I
should never think of refusing them so
small a thing, while to turn one away with
a harsh reply would seem cruel indeed. I
believe we should encourage in our child-
ren a love for flowers and music, and all
such things, which bear refining influences,
and tend to elevate their young minds and
draw them heavenward. Too many peo-
ple who are well-to-do, are too much afraid
of taking a little trouble or going to the
least expense to gratify other children than
their own. But this is no part of our re-
ligion and should not be the feelings of a
Latter-day Saint.
	Many people are poor, and not yet able to
have fine flower gardens. But this is no
reason why their children should not be
taught to appreciate beautiful flowers. I
would teach them that roses are expensive,
but not to be ashamed to ask for them. We
go down on our knees before the Lord and
ask Him for gifts both greater and smaller,
and why not ask favors of each other, and
grant to each other such blessings as we
have in our power to bestow?
	I should consider being called from my
work to give a child a rose, the merest
trifle. As to your being called on such an
errand twenty times a day, I think that in
this expression you have gravely exaggera-
ted.
	CRITIC.


Don't, above all things, walk smoking
by the side of women. No matter if she
does give you permission when you ask it. 
We don't wish you to do it you may be sure.
It is a disrespectful thing. It partakes of
the nature of an insult. No matter how
grand or learned or distingushed you may
be don't do it.
					—Gail Hamilton.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


UNISON.

	Jesus has said "If ye are not one ye are
not mine." Although we may consider our-
selves true Latter-day Saints, if we are not
united in doing that which has been taught
us by those holding the Priesthood we can-
not claim to be one. Are we one in faith?
Yes. Are we one in baptism? Yes, we can
all see that alike. Do we all have faith in
the healing ordinance? A great many, if
not all of us have: we all should have.
Then in what are we not one? In worldly
goods. Have we anything that the Lord
did not give unto us? From what other
source did we derive the blessings that we
daily enjoy? Why do we have health and
life? Why are we not cripples as many of
our fellow beings are? Is it because we
have the ability to take better care of our-
selves than others have? I don't think so;
I think the Lord in His infinite goodness
and mercy gives us life health and ability
to serve Him, and to do good to others and
assist in building up and establishing His
kingdom upon the earth, for this last privi-
lege we should be especially thankful.
Then if He has done all this for us will He
not require obedience at our hands, to the
principles that are taught us from time to
time by His servants which are taught us
for our own benefit, not theirs? Are we
united on all these subjects? If we were
we would have more power to do good;
wher there is Union there is strength.
The Lord can take from us at any moment
life, health and prosperity. He can sweep
all before Him in one breath as it were,
and still He allows us to live on in obedi-
ence or disobedience, whichever path we
choose.
	Will He always be merciful? He has
said that the wicked shall be swept from
the earth; if they do not repent and serve
Him. He has said that the kingdom shall
not be given to another people. But a few
will stand firm and true to the covenants
they have made with God and each other,
and will assist in the redemption of Zion,
but we will only do it by being willing to
make every sacrifice that is necessary to
the accomplishment of that end. We may
have to do many things that are not pleas-
ant, we must not say we cannot, we must
say that we will serve God, come what
may; come life or death; and earn the re-
ward in store for the faithful. We may
have to lay aside the follies and fashions of
the world that have come into our midst.
Shall we say, I CANNOT; if we do, the Lord
will take it from us; far better do it willing-
ly and cheerfully and gain the reward for
so doing; for the Lord has said "My people
shall be willing in the day of my power."
	That we may be humble and faithful, is
the prayer of your sister in the Gospel.
	C. R.


STORING GRAIN.

	RICHFIELD, SEVIER Co., April 5th, 1877.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I will endeavor to give some information
concerning the progress of our Society; we
assure you that the sisters of Richfield are
not altogether dead, although so long un-
heard from. We read with surprise the in-
structions, when first given through the
EXPONENT, for the Relief Societies to Store
up Grain, and our hearts were filled with a
a true desire to obey the call from the ven-
erable leader of our church. It is a proof of
his confidence in the sisters, and we ought
to rise up in our might, yet with all humil-


ity, to engage in this noble enterprise, re-
membering that thereby we shall be able to
make restitution for past wrongs, for had
we, as a general thing, been obedient and
wise, more could have been accomplished
than has been, towards building up Zion.

	HOLDEN, WILLIARD CO., May 21, '77.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We esteem it a privilege to contribute to
your valuable paper, which in perusing we
find very interesting and encouraging, more
especially the situations of the different
Relief Societies throughout the Territory,
with whom we wish ourselves numbered.
We are endeavoring to carry out the coun-
sel of our beloved President Brigham
Young, in laying up grain, planting out
mulberry-trees and encouraging home man-
ufacture as much as lays in our power, us-
ing every advantage possible. Our facili-
ties for raising grain are not so great as
some other localities, yet we intend with
the help of our Heavenly Father, to perse-
vere, and gain the reward allotted to the
faithful.
	We hold our meetings monthly, and ex-
press our feelings and the gratitude of our
hearts for the many blessings we as a peo-
ple receive from time to time.
	We also had our annual meeting March
16 which day we celebrated, commencing
with the usual exercises (singing and pray-
er) after which the annual statements were
read showing the attendance, donations
and labor performed to be very creditable;
and now have on hand quilts, lumber,
cash and grain, amounting to five hundred
dollars.
	Occasionally we are visited by President
Thomas Callister and a few of our leading
Elders who are deeply interested in the
prosperity of the cause of Zion and for
which we are laboring; they impart many
very valuable and impressive instructions to
us.
	Yours very Truly,
		EMMA J. BENNETT.


CORRESPONDENCE.

					MANTI, MAY 24, 1877.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Feeling a deep interest in the silk cul-
ture, and having been accustomed to the
business from my earliest childhood, I 
thought if I could say a word to encourage
those of my sisters who are starting out in
this branch of Home-Industry, I should be
most happy.
	In the first place I will say that I am sat-
isfied by experience, that Utah is quite as
favorable for silk raising as Old New Eng-
land, my native land, and in my young
days there was a great deal made there.
Beside this, it has the advantage of being
a healthy occupation for young or old. I
would say to the young married sisters,
have mulberry trees set out; if you will do
this, in a few years you will have some-
thing for your family to depend upon, which
will supply you with many of the comforts
of life, without applying to your husbands
for means, and will help to make you inde-
pendent in little things and perhaps able to
render assistance in many ways. I have 
heard it remarked, and very truly that 
President Young lives in an atmosphere
above us; I know if we had carried out


his counsel in regard to setting out mul-
berry trees, as well as in everything else,
we should be in a very different condition
to-day. The women of Utah are different
from many of those in the world. We
wish to be helpmeets to our husbands in
very deed. We are willing to spend our
time and the talents, ability and strength
which God has given us in doing good.
	I think we should cultivate that noble
principle of self-reliance that our Father
has implanted in our bosoms, and also in-
struct our children that if they have any-
thing they must earn it, and in time to
come they will bless us for it.
	We feel that we are a blessed people in
Manti, in having the privilege of assisting
to build a Temple in our midst. The sis-
ters here are determined to do what they
can in that direction. We have a fine large
meeting house in process of erection, un-
der the direction of our esteemed Bishop,
J. B. Maiben, who is ever alive to the
wants of the people and willing to encour-
age them in every good move and work.
	Wishing success to the Exponent, I will
close for the present.
	Your sister in the Gospel,
		R. WAREHAM.


RUNAWAY MARRIAGES.

	In a great majority of cases, a lady's elope-
ment is unwise, giddy, ungrateful, immod-
est, and evinces a lascivious appetite and
reckless disposition. Why should she de-
sert and distress those who have loved, nur-
tured and cherished her through all her past
years to throw herself into the arms of a
comparative stranger, who has done noth-
ing for her, and whose protestations of af-
fection have yet to undergo the first trial?
It is every way unworthy of pure and gen-
tle maidenhood to do so.
	We can imagine but one excuse for
her elopement, namely—the efforts of par-
ents or guardians to coerce her into marry-
ing some one she does not love. To avoid
such a fate, she is justified in running 
away, for no parent has or ever had a right
to constrain a daughter to marry against
her will. But where the parents are will-
ing to wait, the daughter should also con-
sent to wait, until her choice is assented to,
or she attains her legal majority. Then if
she chooses to marry in opposition to her
parent's wishes, let her quit her home open-
ly, frankly, in broad daylight and in such a
manner as shall kindly but utterly preclude
any pretense that her act is clandestine or
ill-considered. No one should be persuad-
ed or coerced to marry where she does not
love, but to wait a year or two for the as-
sent of those who have all their life done
what they could for her welfare, no daugh-
ter should esteem a hardship.
	There is some truth to be told about the
"common run" of masculine prowlers by
night, about garden walls and under bed-
room windows, in quest of opportunities to
pour seducing flatteries into the ears of
simple misses; but we have not time to tell
it now. As a general rule they are licen-
tious, good for nothing adventurers, who
would much rather marry a living than
work for it, and who speculate on the chan-
ces of "bringing the old folks round" after
a year or two. A true man would not ad-
vise, much less urge, the woman he loved, to
take a step which must inevitably lessen
the respect felt for her, and violate the truth
reposed in her by those who had loved and
cherished her all her days.—Home Circle.


16		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


FLOWERS.

	NOT only is kindness due to every one,
but a special kindness is due to every one.
Kindness is not kindness unless it be spe-
cial. It is in its fitness, seasonablesness, and
individual application that its charm con-
sists.

	HE that wrestles with us strengthens our
nerves and sharpens our skill. Our antag-
onist is our helper. This amiable conflict
with difficulty forces upon us an intimate
acquaintance with our object, and compels
us to consider it in all its relations. It will
not suffer us to be superficial.

	YOUNG men are as apt to think themselves
wise enough as drunken men are to think
themselves sober enough. They look upon
spirit to be a much better thing than experi-
ence, which they call coldness. They are
but half mistaken; for, though spirit with-
out experience is dangerous, experience
without spirit is languid and ineffective.—
Chesterfield.

	AGE AND YOUTH.—Age that is tender
and maternal, age that remembers its own
youth and does not demand bread from the
growing corn, nor wine from the green
grapes, and youth that can feel for infirmi-
ties which it does not know and that can
believe in wisdom which it has not reached
—these two together make a whole, perfect
for what it is, and turn a chance companion-
ship into a mutual blessing, and out of a
temporary association create a life-long
affection.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	Bare Neck and Arms.—An eminent physician
declared: "I believe that during the twenty-six
years I have followed my profession in this city,
twenty thousand children have been carried to the
cemeteries, a sacrifice to the absurd custom of ex-
posing their arms naked. And yet it is said the
low-neck fashion is coming in again. Do not fol-
low it.
	Mental Development.—It is coming to be well
known that mental development is the result of
properly directed physical training; that the brain
grows in size and power by the varied exercise of
the senses and the will in mechanical employments
quite as rapidly as by purely intellectual efforts in
study or otherwise. It is equally known to phy-
siologists that most men are one-sided in heads as
in their bodies. The two halves of the brain are
rarely developed symmetrically, as may be readily
seen in the "conforms," or head measures accum-
ulated by hatmakers supplying individual custom-
ers. To some extent the difference in the contour
of the two sides of the head may be due to un-
equal pressure on the nurse's arm, or to the habit
of lying chiefly on the one side while sleeping,
thus causing a permanent displacement of the
walls of the skull, but the main reason appears to
be our one-sided habit of education. Dr. Brown
Sequard observes that the study of the facts in re-
lation to the brain has led him to believe that
"each half of the brain—paradoxical as it may
seem—is a whole brain."—Scientific American.
	Failures and Health.—The effect of so much
financial trouble is, for the time, very distressing
on the health of those involved. Debts and ina-
bility to meet them depress vital energy and im-
pair physical integrity; hence we see so many tak-
ing their own lives. The brain reels, the nervous
system totters and falls, and the man is a wreck
who once was the noblest work of God. There
is little hope of the generation now on the stage
that they will ever all learn the laws of true living


so as to be able to stand up against misfortune, or,
better still, avoid it; but there is a better time
coming, when people will study the laws of life as
revealed in our bodies and minds, and then things
will be different. They will then be so instructed
that they will refuse to enter upon a course of life
calculated to end in ruin to body and soul.
	Those in business will do well to heed the signs
of the times, and to guard the body from harm by
right living, and to keep their business within the
limits of their ability, so in times of pressure to
be able to endure. There can be no great and
abiding success in business enterprises without
sound health, good bodies, firm nerves, a stomach
capable of doing its daily duty, and a nervous sys-
tem that can throw off care at night, and take
sweet, serene repose. Look well to these things,
and there will be little to fear.—Herald of Health.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	To preserve winter clothing from moths, the
very best method is to wrap each article in brown
paper. It has been proven one of the best pre-
ventives from moths in the world. Stiff seam-
less sacks are excellent to keep out moths, and
put in the sack some camphor and tobacco.
	Dough-nuts: One pint of flour, one table-spoon-
ful of sugar, one teaspoonful of cream of tartar,
half teaspoonful of soda, and two eggs. Mix
well with milk; roll out the paste, and cut it out in
any shape. Fry in boiling lard until a nice brown.
