WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, FEBRUARY 15, 1878.	No. 18


[For the EXPONENT.
		HURRYING HOME.

Hurrying home in the twilight's gray hour,
As night spreads her mantle o'er cottage and tower;
Home from the toils and the cares of the day,
And they at its threshold should vanish away.

Some pass along with a quick, joyous tread,
And some from whose pathway life's pleasures hath fled,
For some there are greetings of love and delight,
And others the shadow of sorrow's deep night.

And I read in each face as they pass swiftly by,
The light, happy heart, the tear and the sigh;
Can tell where the home-lights shine out through the
	gloom;
Can tell where the hearth-stone is cold as the tomb.

Children's fair faces pass swiftly along,
Like sunbeams they glance 'mid the hurrying throng;
Children so innocent, joyous and bright,
Oh, fain would I shield you from shadows of night.

Be not too eager to reach manhood's prime,
Childhood's the fairest, the sunniest clime;
Cling to its freshness, cling to its truth,
And gather sweet fruits from the garden of youth.

Here too I see the feeble and old;
Soon they'll be gathered to Christ and the fold;
Home-light will darken for them nevermore,
But ever burn bright on the "Golden Shore."

"No home" is stamped on this pitiful face,
In all this wide world she hath no place;
Ye who have homes, oh, think of the poor;
In mercy remember, turn not from thy door.

And draw not the curtains too closely, I pray,
But out through the gloom let the cheering light stray;
It may fall on some weary and desolate heart,
Who of life's blessings hath had meager part.

"Home, home sweet home," more precious than gold,
Half of thy value can never be told.
Make your homes "Rests" 'mid the toil and the strife,
Oasis green in the journey of life.
									HOPE.
			S. E. [---]sell


[Column 1]
			[For the EXPONENT.
TWO PAIRS OF HANDS.

	What! Have hands a history? yes; and
if you will put down your work and come
and sit beside me, I will relate to you their
story. You see those hands laid with such
exquisite grace on the black velvet and
cushion? Ah, yes; how soft and delicately
molded is each curve and outline. Thefingers taper to the waxy, transparent nail,
and the pink tips are just formed to press
the ivory of the piano. The fleah is white
and satin smooth. "How beautiful!" you
are led to exclaim, "are the hands of a fair
and delicate woman." As they lie there
in their fair beauty, bend the ear of your
imagination, and hear them tell their story:
	"We were cared for with all the tender-
ness a fond mistress could bestow on that
which adds so much to her appearnce in
society. The kid has been shorn, the finest
silk has been sought for, to protect us from
the sun and wind. Our owner would never
think of placing us to do that work which
belongs to the kitchen wench. Look at us
in our beauty! What good have we ever
accomplished? To be sure we glide softly
over the piano keys, or perchance fashion
some delicate garment for our owner. We
are taught to intertwine gracefully to en-
hance the glitter of the diamonds with
which we are adorned. We attract atten-
tion to our mistress, and help her to secure
to herself that admiration and flattery
which is the life of her life and aim of her

[Column 2]
soul. Oh, stay; you ask us what good we
shall have been the means of doing our
mistress in the next world, and what use
we shall be to her in the great eternity to
come? Oh, bless our hearts! why do you
suggest such disagreeable thoughts? Well,
our mistress says she is living for this
world, and if this life, which is only a drop
in the great sea of eternity, is all that she
cares for, we shall not trouble ourselves
about anything else. And when death
comes, we shall make a very lovely sight
to the mortal eye, crossed over the heart
that knew no higher aim than an assidious
care for us. We shall repay her at least."
	Ah, my friend, you turn away with a
sickening disgust from these white, useless
hands. Well, I blame you not. Just look
now at this pair of hard, seamed, dark
hands. The fingers firmly interlocked,
showing the force of character in her whose
life has been made up of toil. We will
listen also to them, and take to our hearts
and lives the lesson they will teach.
	"We are the servants of her who never
spends a moment upon us, but to cleanse
and dry the skin. We are always em-
ployed; no moment is laid on the altar of
time unless used in some way. Besides the
usual daily housework, we fashion a useful,
comfortable bonnet for some old lady friend;
put together in dainty form a hood to frame
a sweet baby face. We strive to help our
owner to make that which we handle dainty
and delicate, and to spare no toil to make
her home happy and cheerful for her moth-
er and two precious fatherless boys, rather
than seek to preserve our beauty at the ex-
pense of others' hearts and hopes. Yet
when sickness prostrates those around our
owner we are guided with loving tender-
ness over the suffering brow, and many an
agonized lip has pronounced our touch the
most soothing mortal can know. And we
are taken to the kitchen, to prepare that
for the sick which will comfort the body
while our gentle pressure soothes the throb-
bing brow. We have accompanied our mis-
tress to many a stricken home, and are
never tired of doing good. We will con-
fess to you, in strict confidence, that some-
times she whom we serve with such dili-
gence will look at us with discontent, and
actually murmur at our plainness. Yes, it
was painful, after we had done so much to
secure eternal happiness for her, and to
hear her complain of us and wish we were
otherwise. But then we forgave the weak-
ness and still served her faithfully. The
next world? Ah, indeed, we have no fear
of that; and when we are folded above the
heart which has never known guile, we
shall await with peace our call to come forth.
And there we will be honored for our good
works on this earth, and blessed by those
to whom we have ministered in this life."
	Are not those hands lovely to you now,
my friend? They are to me, and I some-
times reverently kiss them for the noble
work they are doing.			S. Y. D.
	St. George, Jan. 28.


IT IS said that it costs $177,444 a year to
supply the American army with tobacco.
"The extravagance of women is ruining
the country!"


NOTES AND NEWS.

	MRS. EMMA Gates Conklin is the editor
of the New York "Palladium," a monthly
publication, "devoted to Literature, Poetry,
Music, and the best interests of our coun-
try—socially, morally, and politically."
	"DO ALL women vote in the cities of
England, or only tax-payers and house-
renters?" asks a subscriber. Only unmar-
ried women and widows who pay a tax on
property or who rent a house can vote,
these being the same qualifications as are
required of male voters.
	MRS. SOPHIE B. Herrick, whose micro-
scopical studies, published in "Scribner's
Monthly" during the past year, have at-
tracted wide attention, has become perma-
nently connected with the editorial depart-
ment of that Magazine, and is now widely
recognized as standing in the first rank of
American scientific women. A touch of
her quality can be found in the review of
Joseph Cook's "Biology," recently pub-
lished in "Scribner."
	PARLIAMENT MET on Friday, Jan. 17th.
The Queen did not open the session in per-
son, but sent her speech to be read. It is
mainly about the Eastern War. She says,
"Hitherto, neither of the belligerents have
infringed the conditions on which my neu-
trality is founded, and I willingly believe
both parties are desirous to respect them,
so far as it may be in their power. So long
as these conditions are not infringed, my
attitude will continue the same. No efforts
on my part will be wanting to promote a
pacific solution of the points at issue and
the termination of the war." She solicits
the liberality of Parliament to supply means
for adequate preparation and to adopt mea-
sures of precaution, should hostilities be
unfortunately prolonged.
	In the debates on the Address in the
House of Lords, Earl Granville called Lord
Beaconsfield to account for making utter-
ances during the recess in a tone of bellig-
erent neutrality, and for differing from the
sentiments expressed by his colleagues;
said if Russia's assurances were unsatisfac-
tory, Parliament should have been called
together immediately; but if satisfactory,
summoning Parliament now was to cause a
feeling of insecurity. To which Lord Bea-
consfield replied, that when the summons to
Parliament was issued, there were pros-
pects of peace negotiations; he repudiated
what the newspapers say about the policy
of government, and emphatically what
they say about a divided cabinet; he de-
clared he had written no letter during re-
cess, and had spoken but once, and then in
the presence of his colleagues; that Lord
Derby's felicitous statement, that "the
greatest British interest is Peace," was a
rhetorical expression, but Lord Granville
seemed to take it as a statistical fact; that
the government considered that there were
British interests which must not be endan-
gered if the contest goes on. There was
another kind of isolation than that arising
from decay. England once alone asserted
her national independence. The Duke of
Argyle said that Lord Beaconsfield's speech
was brilliant and evasive.—


138		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
R. S. REPORTS.

RESIDENCE ELDER JOHN PACK,
					Jan. 17, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Minutes of the Meeting convened to or-
ganize the young ladies of the 17th Ward
into a Mutual Improvement Association.
	Present, by invitation, to assist in organ-
izing the young sisters, Miss E. R. Snow,
Mrs. Hyde, President of the Relief Society,
with her counselor, Mrs. S. Reese, and other
assistants in office. Meeting conduced in
the usual manner by singing and prayer;
after which Miss E. R. Snow stated the ob-
ject of the meeting, and gave a brief ac-
count of the Juvenile Society of the ward.
Said "about four years ago, some little
girls went to the Bishop, and requested to
be organized into a Society; their request
was treated somewhat coldly, as the Bishop
did not understand what they asked of
him. However they persevered, and went
a second time. It is presumed that the
Bishop had seen Pres. Young concerning
their request, in the meantime, for he told
them that they could have the Meeting
House to hold meetings in at any time, and
blessed them.
	"I always admired that move, and to-day
bless those girls. But now it seems wis-
dom that the older sisters engage in this
organization. Many of the young sisters
have had experience in this matter, yet the
older ones should take the lead, that all
might be combined and work together. I
think the young married ladies should be-
long to this Association, and every girl as
soon as she is baptized. The organization
of these Associations is as much a revela-
tion as that of the Relief Societies; the one
came through Joseph Smith, the other
through Brigham Young, and if I was to
choose between the two organizations, I
should choose the one for the young, so many
of whom, for the want of such spiritual cul-
ture, have gone to destruction.
	"When we first came here there were no
regular schools; but as soon as we obtained
the necessaries of life, attention was turned
to educating the children mentally, but as
they were born in the Church, and heirs by
right to the kingdom, no thought was be-
stowed upon their spiritual culture.
	"The world educate their children to the
highest mental standpoint within their
reach. But we had come out of Babylon,
and thought we had left her behind; but
the spirit of the world had crept in among
our young people, and we had Infidels
among them, children of good parents; and
the question was asked, 'What will become
of our children?' and no one knew, until
the Lord spoke, and we were commanded
to organize and teach them.
	"We were met with scorn, but we had
our mission, and we have worked from then
until now. Many of the societies have
broken up, yet some have remained true to
their integrity; we knew the result must
be good, for the work was from God.
	"At first many of the girls were laughed
at for attending meetings, and scorned by
their associates; others met with more en-
couragement; the mothers became inter-
ested, when they saw the result, for now
the girls could testify that the work was
true; before they supposed it was, for their
parents said so: now they knew it for
themselves. So far the results have far
exceeded our expectations, and there are
many splendid Secretaries among the young
ladies.
	"On one occasion Pres. Young met with
us in our General Meeting, and heard the

[Column 2]
young ladies read their minutes, and he
remarked: 'Two-thirds of the men in Con-
gress could do no better than that.'
	"Pres. Young gave Elder Junius Wells
the mission to go through the Territory
and organize the young men; but the young
ladies have got the start of them, and
know better how to conduct their meetings;
but the young men are progressing and
moving steadily forward.
	"I want you young ladies to become quali-
fied that when the queens of the earth come
here, you can instruct them in the princi-
ples of the Gospel. In your meetings have
good reading—selections from good books—
and I must say many of the selections I
have heard read were as good as I could
make myself.
	"In the early days of the Church, the
young took delight in the work, and it was
no uncommon thing for the children to
speak in tongues, and have the blessings of
the Gospel poured upon them. But chil-
dren get no more than they earn, either of
the spirit of the world, or the Spirit of God.
No one can get the knowledge of God any
cheaper than Peter got it, when Christ said
'Flesh and blood did not reveal this unto
thee, but my Father who is in heaven.' I
remember in the early days some little
girls met together to hold meeting. They
sang and prayed and appointed their
speakers; but as they were sincere, the
Lord poured out His Spirit upon them and
they spoke in tongues and were so happy
that they had to tell of it.
	"Now some one must preside over this
Association, and I see before me many
young ladies who could preside with digni-
ty, if they would exercise their ability,
aided by the Spirit of God. We can use
any one who is willing to do His will. Wo-
man's duties are increasing year by year,
therefore, my sisters, prepare yourselves
for high and honorable callings. I propose
Miss Belle Clayton as President of this As-
sociation, but none of you need vote for
her, if you do not think this a wise choice."
	After some deliberation it was moved
and passed without a dissenting vote, that
Miss Belle Clayton be President, and Mrs.
Maria Dougall, Miss Ida Felt, Miss Phoebe
Tingey, and Miss Emmerette Brown were
nominated and sustained as her Counselors;
also Mrs. Josephine Smith, Secretary, and
Miss Denie Pack, Assistant Secretary of the
Association; Miss Lucy Smith, Treasurer,
and Miss Hortense Lang, Treasurer of the
"Penny Fund" for the Temple. All these
officers were elected by unanimous vote.
	Mrs. Julia Howe who had been Assistant
Superintendent of the Juvenile Society,
said she felt as if these young girls were
her own children, for they had been asso-
ciated together so long and she had learned
to love them dearly. Said they had done
well; these little girls had actually made
thirty yards of carpet, which had been do-
nated to the Temple. Said she should al-
ways be interested in their welfare, and
should miss their society.
	Twenty young sisters came forward and
their names were enrolled as members of
the Association.
	Pres. Mrs. Hyde said she was much
pleased with the proceedings to-day, and
felt that the President chosen was the right
one, and added her blessing, and prayed
that God would give his Spirit to guide the
young.
	Sisters R. M. Carrington, J. Pack, M. A.
Pratt, H. Doremus, J. Smith, Denie Pack,
Ida Felt, Phebe Tingey, Hortense Lang,
Edith Smith, Melvy Calder, Ella Dallas. E.
Groesbeck, Mary P. Young, Eliza Kettle,
S. Reese, Vinnie Clayton, and L. D. Alder

[Column 3]
each made some wise and interesting re-
marks to the young ladies, encouraging
them to move forward in their new organ-
ization.
	Sister Snow advised them to be punctual
at their meetings, and make them short
but interesting.
	Pres. Miss Clayton appointed the next,
meeting for the young ladies at her house
on Wednesday afternoon of next week at 3
o'clock.
	Pres. Mrs. Hyde adjourned the Relief
meeting until two weeks from to-day at 2
o'clock. Meeting closed with singing and
prayer.
		MRS. L. D. ALDER, Secretary.

	MANTI, SANPETE Co., Jan. 24th, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	It is not often that we occupy any space
in your much esteemed paper, but I hope
that a short report of the doings of the
sisters of our ward will not be unaccepta- 
ble. The building of the Temple and new
meeting house in our midst, calls for our
best energies, and the sisters feel that a
certain amount of the responsibilities thus
created naturally falls upon them, and are
ready to respond freely and voluntarily to
the demands of the times. . It is, therefore,
gratifying to be able to report the flourish-
ing condition of all the enterprises we have-
undertaken. Under the guidance of our
much beloved and esteemed President,
Sister R. Wareham, we have commenced 
storing grain, and have already several
hundred bushels on hand. Our Society
Store is also doing well, having doubled
our capital from the time we started, about
eighteen months ago. This ought to speak
well for the management, and the interest
the sisters take in it. The general authori-
ties have the full confidence of the com-
munity, good feeling prevails, and a spirit 
of generous rivalry pervades all; our pre-
siding sister has determined that the gen-
tler sex shall not be behind in fulfilling the
duties and meeting the responsibilities with
which the times are pregnant. And thus
the good cause moves on, and we look for-
ward with anticipations of a glorious fu-
ture. 
	It is with extreme sorrow that we chroni-; 
cle the demise of one of our members, Sis-
ter Maria Bench, who was interred to-day.
She was an exemplary wife, good member,
and always punctual to the duties of the
hour, and her family have the sympathy
of the whole community. "Peace to her
ashes."
	Your sister in the covenant,
							W.C.


﻿IN THE city of Haverhill, Mass., several
hundred women pay taxes on $559,480.
They are going to petition the Legislature
for a right to vote in municipal elections.


NOTICE.

	All who may wish to purchase silk-worm
eggs can obtain them from Mrs. Dora Robinson
of Farmington. It would be well for those
who intend to purchase to secure what quanti-
ty they may need, and if desirable they could
leave them in Mrs. Robinson’s care till it was
time to make use of them. Mulberry trees of
all sizes can be obtained from President
Young's farm, just south of the city, by apply-
ing to Wm. Rossiter, at the President’s Office.
The trees are from five to forty cents apiece,
according to size.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[For the EXPONENT.
		ESTRANGED.

And hast thou shut and locked thy heart
	Against me? Nay, not so.
Whom once I loved, I ever love;
	I cannot let thee go.
Thou, who hast dwelt within my love,
	Winning thy place so well:
Ah! must we say goodbye to hearts?
	I cannot say farewell.

Thou, who alone didst watch my bed
	Of sorrow, pain and fear;
While wintry night raged dark and wild,
	And death seemed all too near.
Can I forget those dream-like days,
	When, resting in thy care,
I traced the wanderings of thy songs,
	Upon the charmed air?

E'en if some idle word let fall,
	(As leaves float on the wind)
Long wandering, to thy gentle heart
	Its way at last did find,
Ah! who would weigh it 'gainst the past,
	With all its memories dear?
Not thou, or I, who know so well,
	Life's holy mission here.

Ah! who would take the perfect rose,
	Love on its heart had worn,
And counting not it's loveliness,
	Treasure alone the thorn?
I could not sing in heaven, if there
	A loved face turned away,
Unreconciled; 'twould chill my joy,
	E'en in that perfect day.

Though life be long, and earth be wide,
	All vain to turn away;
We oft shall meet, amid that throng,
	Who walk the narrow way.
When we shall meet beside that gate,
	Thou wilt not answer no;
Thou'lt know with joy my patient faith--
	For I have loved thee so.
Jan. 1, 1878.		AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.


[Column 1]
			[For the EXPONENT.
A VISIT TO ORDERVILLE.

	The day dawned bright and beautiful. It
was the Sabbath. I had looked forward to
this day, desiring to visit my dear Colima's
grave. The sun had warmed the air. I
started from Glendale, with my little boy
to keep me company. In the twelve last
years, since I left there, everything had
changed, except the everlasting hills. The
road ran on the east side of the valley in-
stead of the west, and all the crossings of
the creek were different. We walked up
the dugway, through a small but fragrant
grove of cedars, and crossed a creek. Noth-
ing familiar here told of the fatal accident
that called my child so suddenly into the
land of spirits. No familiarity of scene or
associations called to my mind the bitter
memory of the past, which connected me
in thought and feeling to the place. Over
ploughed ground and grassy pastures we
continued our way, through shrubs and
willows, where cattle grazed all unconscious
of my aching heart and the terrible re-
membrances awakened by nearing the place
of death; a small stream of water was
crossed, and before me was the knoll where
my beloved child was buried. I hastened
to the grave; it was concealed by shrub-
bery growing in wild profusion, and
smoothed by the hand of time, but the
mother's memory always discerns quickly
such precious earth-spots. With tears and
prayers I consecrated the spot anew, and
after an hour spent at the sacred place on
this holy Sabbath day, we proceeded on
our way to Orderville. As we rounded the
Yellow Mountain we came in sight of Or-
derville. Then we crossed a field and
passed by the vineyard and we found our-
selves at the village.
	I had some strange impressions as I
looked at the long line of low houses built

[Column 2]
of wood, and thought what havoc the fire-
fiend could make among them.
	I stopped at the house of a friend, who
was delighted to see me, and she exclaimed
in her amazement, "If you had dropped
from the skies I should not have been more
surprised."
	After a lively chat, asking and answer-
ing questions, she went out and soon re-
turned with a warm meal ready to serve.
"See how quickly I've got dinner," she
said; "its no trouble here; all you have to
do is to go to the cook-room and it is all
ready." To my inquiries of How do you
like the United Order? the answer was
from those interrogated, "I would not live
out of it for the world." Sister Maria took
me to the dining-room at sundown to see
the children at the table. There were three
long tables filled with happy, healthy, mer-
ry children, whispering and talking low
among themselves, waiting the arrival of
Sister Harmon. She entered, pronounced
the one word "Silence," and all were still.
The blessing was asked, instructions in po-
liteness given, and Sister Harmon said:
"Children, you must not throw a crumb
of bread under the table, or waste any-
thing, you know that famine is coming."
Contentedly the little ones ate their plain,
simple food and quietly passed out.
	I met one after another of friends and
acquaintances, who expressed surprise and
pleasure, until the emotions of the day al-
most overcame me. A few hours before I
had stood by the grave of my beloved child,
now I was meeting old and tried friends,
awakening memories and incidents of trial,
fatigue, sorrow, joy, gladness, all inter-
mingled in the remembrances that crowded
upon me. I met two strangers, who, by
their kindly greeting and sisterly manner,
seemed like old friends. Many varied
scenes crowded close one upon another,
crowned by this last—the glimpse of life
in Orderville. While chatting with Myra,
another dear friend, Maria, entered, and
asked me if I would like to go in to prayers.
I assented gladly, and followed her to the
dining-room. The tables were fast filling
with men and women, old, middle-aged
and young. When all were seated a hymn
was sung, then a prayer offered, and all
partook of the same plain fare as the chil-
dren had just had served to them. So
many persons gathered from all places, with
such a variety of tastes and habits, partak-
ing of the same kind of food, discarding
tea and coffee, and abjuring the proverb
that "What is one man's meat is another's
poison." After greeting and interchanges
of the kindliest feelings, I looked round
the room, and my heart swelled within me.
I was really among those who had estab-
lished and were carrying out an Order of
union, as near perfect and complete as
seemed possible in this state of existence.
The feeling of love and union that existed
there was strongly akin to the heavenly
feeling in the St. George Temple, and I re-
alized that some day, if they keep the same
spirit, they will be among the mighty and
wealthy of the earth.
	Supper over the room was cleared and
prepared for the evening meeting, which
was well attended—preaching by Mission-
aries from Kanab. After the meeting was
over, seated around a bright cedar fire,
which sparkled and glowed, filling the
room with warmth and light, we talked of
the past, present and future.
	That night after retiring, sleep disdained
to visit me until after twelve, for busy
thoughts were my companions. Morning
came; I breakfasted with my kind hostess,
who had been ill and was not yet recovered

[Column 3]
sufficiently to go to the general table, but
got her breakfast ready cooked. Doing all
the cooking in one place is an admirable
contrivance to save woman's time, instead
of a hundred getting breakfast separately;
a few do that work, and the time of the
rest can be employed advantageously at
other work.
	After breakfast we visited the cook-room.
The baker was making mush to mix with
salt-rising bread to eke out the flour, until
the milling was done. They have warm
bread for breakfast made with hop-yeast,
and salt-rising for dinner and supper. A
large boiler full of beef was in progress of
cooking, and two boys were preparing po-
tatoes for dinner, cabbages were waiting to
be cooked, dry bread was gathered up for
soup, a little boy was picking over raisins,
and dinner was being prepared for the
many. I saw the steamer in which the po-
tatoes are cooked over large boilers, or fur-
naces; the large, bright range, with its
capacity for cooking large quantities of food
and keeping it warm; the happy counten-
ances of the people in the next room at
breakfast, and I felt that here was a begin-
ning of something new—a step in the right
direction.
	The little that I saw I liked. Of course
the Order has both enemies and friends,
and there are many outside ready to find
fault and pick flaws, but my prayer is, may
the spirit of union and peace spread abroad,
until "The Order" will be the fashion, and
out of the order the exception.
					EMILY B. SPENCER.
	St. George.


COMMENTS ON CONGRESS-MEN.

	If Mary Clemmer's article in the New
York "Independent," is a true statement
of the Senatorial behavior, on receiving
Petitions from all the States for a Sixteenth
Amendment to the Constitution in favor
of Woman's Suffrage; the great majority of
that body fell into that trap, which a just
Providence permits to be set to divulge cor-
ruption. The manner in which the peti-
tions were received by the Senate, tells the
tale of the moral condition of that body.
	Men devoid of self-respect, do not re-
spect women. Want of true self-respect
bespeaks immorality; and immorality in
men, engenders in them disrespect for wo-
men.
	The very cause of this remarkable dem-
onstration, of petitions from all the states
and territories in the Union, is certainly a
full-grown corruption in the land; and the
petitions, an anti-act, against corruption.
The sagacious and heroic representatives of
a great reform, are bravely facing the very
centre of corruption, on its own premises,
meeting its most formidable foe in its own
chambers, to fight the battle on the enemy's
ground. Then and there commenced the
real battle, compared with which the past
has only been skirmishing.
	The insulted petitioners should have sent
detectives at the heels of those eminently
rude actors of the Senate, got possession of
a few details of their behavior "behind the
scene," and exposed it to the public gaze.
That would be a firm tactic, to commence
with, in crushing a powerful foe.
	But it is perhaps fortunate as it is; it will
rouse the energy of the petitioners, who
have backing enough not to relax their ef-
forts, in so good a cause, be their opposers
who they may.
							H. M.

							Salt Lake City, Utah.

	SALT LAKE CITY, FEB. 15, 1878.


[Column 1]
		WOMAN’S PROGRESSION.

	The newspapers of the day among other items
of importance bring us good news of the pro-
gression of Woman's Cause. The past year has
given strength to the work by many large con-
ventions, calling together the strongest and
most powerful minds from different parts of
the country, and these women, earnest and
zealous, have presented in the most forcible
manner the ablest and most direct argu-
ments in favor of woman's elevation. The
right to equal opportutities of education is one
of the points in question, and much has been
done to give woman opportunities for cultiva-
tion in the higher branches of literature re-
cently. But many of those who do not direct
ly oppose it, (knowing woman has the best side
of the argument) sneer, and ridicule and insin-
uate, and in all sorts of indirect ways use their
influence to keep women from pursuing those
avocations that are considered the most eligible
for man.
	The better educated woman is, the more she
can accomplish for the good of all; the better
she can perform the duties of wife and mother
which we consider the highest duty except
obedience to God. One plea that many ad-
vance against woman's higher education, and
freedom, is that it will unfit her for the posi-
tion nature has assigned her as wife and moth-
er. But we sincerely believe it will only the
better qualify her. The mother who has
strong characteristics properly developed will
transmit them to her children in a marked de-
gree. This is a fact readily conceded by every
person of good judgment; then the more high-
ly educated the mothers are, the greater power
of intellect the children will inherit.
	So long has custom tyrannized over woman
to keep her in subjection, that it will only be
by small degrees women themselves can com-
prehend the advantages arising from the pro-
gress of independence of thought and action,
and a knowledge for themselves.
	The fields of usefulness now open to woman,
are widening every day. And there are some
good men—here as elsewhere, who welcome
her to the avenues which lead to a higher
range of intellectual and spiritual culture. But
men cannot walk by faith as women can, it is
not so much in their nature; they want facts
all the time, and the results of practise; and it
will take a little time to prove whether the
condition of society is materially benefitted by
woman's broader expanse of labor in the pub-
lic, as well as private interests of humanity.
Some things have been already proved; that
women are capable of mastering, as thorough-
ly and perfectly as men, the theory and prac-
tice of teaching, the science of medicine and
anatomy, and many other branches of educa-
tion and art. The one strong point that oppo-

[Column 2]
nents use is, that woman will lose her inde-
finable charm, designated womanliness, when
she can discuss knotty questions, has strong
opinions, can match herself with man in force
of character, and intellectual ability, instead
of sweetly assenting to whatever opinions he
may hold, smiling all the while he is pitilessly
crucifying all the finer feelings of her soul.
	Why is it not possible for man and woman
to love each other truly, and dwell together in
harmony, each according to the other all the
freedom of thought, feeling, and expression
they would grant to one who was not bound to
them by indissoluble ties; instead of the wife
giving all—that she may sit by his hearth, bear
his children, preside at his table, and merge
her life into his, to the extinguishing and crush-
ing out of all desires, ambitions, tastes, or capa-
bilities for anything save what he deems pro-
per, or right, his wife should engage in. Let
man also prove himself noble enough to share
with her such laurels as either may be able to
win in the battle field of life, instead of arro-
gating to himself the right to dictate to her in
all things, saying, "thus far shalt thou go and
no farther."
	We do not believe in pouring forth griev-
ances, or reiterating wrongs; these are not ar-
guments, and men will not have them; they
must be shown the happy side of the future,
not the dark annals of the past. That we
must leave—and we know there is One, Who
would not even suffer a sparrow to fall to the
ground without His notice. Jesus never turned
away from woman in scorn, or loathing; and
woman never sued to him in vain. Think of
this, you who sit in the courts of the nation,
and sneer at the mothers, wives and sisters who
entreat you to devise some methods to save
from wholesale destruction, by intemperance
and other crying evils, their sons, their bro-
thers, their loved ones, their fellow-men, or
allow THEM a voice in the laws of the land,
that will at least give them legal rights to pro-
tect themselves and their little ones.
	Of what are ye afraid? Has not woman ex-
tended to you the hand of sympathy, meted
out measure upon measure, pressed down and
running over with love and heavenly charity?
What more can she do to win your confidence
in her desire to improve the condition of all
mankind? Has she not unselfishly sought
your interest in everything which pertains to
home happiness! Will she slacken her efforts
to do you good because she has a wider field to
labor in, and greater inducements to stimulate
her exertions? No! woman's happiness can
never be complete without man's love; but
that love molded and tempered by its assimi-
lation to the exalted phase of woman—with all
her powers and attributes awakened to a su-
preme consciousness of her true mission as
man's real help meet, will give you participa-
tion in a purer sentiment, where there will be
scope for all the love implanted in the breast
of man.
	There is no doubt but that the concentrated
energies and eloquence brought to bear upon
Woman's cause in the National Congress, this
present year will be long remembered, and
even those who profess to think lightly of it,
and treated it with contempt and withering
sarcasm, will not be able to forget the sublime
enthusiasm, the dignity and grace with which
these noble women plead for the rights of
which they are unjustly deprived.
	There are some valiant workers in the cause
of woman's progression—we have them here
in our midst; women who are laboring ear-
nestly in improving and elevating the condi-
tion of society, whose efforts are untiring in
seeking to promote a higher and more exalted

[Column 3]
feeling and sentiment in the minds of the
young, than to spend their time in frivolity
and nonsense. Women who are prepared and
fortified to endure the sneers and opprobrium
of those who do not yet appreciate the cause
they have espoused; who in all humility seek
for the grace of God to aid them, in their ef-
forts to accomplish their noble purposes.
						E. B. Wells


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	The Municipal Election passed off more har-
moniously than appearances seemed to indi-
cate. The People's Ticket carried by a large
majority.
	The "Deseret News" of Tuesday 12 inst. con-
tains among other good items, an interesting
letter from Washington, by C. A. S., an inter-
view between Hon. George Q. Cannon, Dele-
gate from Utah, and the reporter of the Wash-
ington Post. In this encounter Br. Cannon
seems to have spoken very plainly and in lan-
guage not to be misunderstood.
	Dispatches tell us in reference to the death
of Pope Pius IX, that he was attacked with a
sort of suffocation or difficult breathing at 8
o'clock on Wednesday evening, the 8th inst.,
at 11 o'clock he was very much worse; then he
took the crucifix from under his pillow, and
blessed those around him. He afterwards
blessed the Sacred College at the request of a
Cardinal who was present. The Cardinals in
attendance relieved each other in praying by
the bedside. At 3 40, the corpse was livid.
Solemn masses and requiems for the Pope are
being held in large cities throughout the dif-
ferent countries where Roman Catholic Church-
es are established and great pomp and ceremony
are manifested on his account.
	The official report of the physicians who at-
tended the Pope, report the cause of his death
"Paralysis of the lungs." The Pope was to lie
in state three days in the hall of the con[-]i[-]tory,
after the preliminary congregation, which
took place immediately after his death. The
body will lie in the Basilics of St. Peter await-
ing the last ceremony. It is expected the cer-
emonies will continue nine days, after which
the conclave will close.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE UTAH "Musical Bouquet" No 10, Vol.
1, has been received, and we are pleased with
its contents, and gratified to know that among
other home productions, Utah has begun to de-
velop musical talent. We trust the Musical
Bouquet will find many friends and patrons in
the homes of the saints.

	THE UTAH "Musical Times No. 11, Vol. 2, is
an excellent copy. It contains notes of inter-
est on the subject of musical composition, style,
etc, as well as items regarding composers of all
countries. If these periodicals are kept and
bound in volumes they will make a valuable
addition to home literature.

	LECTURES DELIVERED under the auspices
of the Young Men's Mutual Improvement As-
sociation are awakening considerable interest
among the young men. The lecture of Elder
Moses Thatcher published in the "Deseret
News" of Feb. 9th, contains many excellent
ideas, and couched in language simple yet elo-
quent. The lecture is well worth studying as
well as casually reading. But reading a lec-
ture is not as impressive, howover carefully it
may be read as hearing it delivered by a per-
son who feels himself what he speaks, and
throws into his subject the force of his re-


marks. The voice, the manner, the presence
all serve to enhance the value of the opinions
or views expressed.
	ELDER DANIEL Graves has been traveling
and lecturing in the interests of sericulture in
the northern settlements. Elder G. informs
us that he has delivered thirteen lectures dur-
ing his tour. In most places he had very large
audiences and in all there seemed to be a re-
newal of interest on the subjeet of silk culture.
In Centreville they have a ribbon loom, and
have excellent warp, and are waiting for suita-
ble filling. Elder G. is engaged in a good
cause, and one that should be encouraged by
those who are looking to the future interests of
the community. Elder G. states that according
to the statistics on silk importation that in two
ports only, New York and San Francisco, over
$28,000,000 were expended for raw silk in the
year 1875. The subject of sericulture is now
under discussion throughout the Territory, and
there will be multitudes of trees put out this
year.
	THE TELEPHONE:—On Thursday afternoon,
through the courtesy of Elder A. M. Musser,
we had the privilege of witnessing the trial of
that recent invention, for transmitting sound,
the Telephone. It is seemingly of very simple
construction. The connection was from the
office of President Taylor to that of Bishop
Hunter, nearly a block, and the sounds were
heard perfectly when all was quiet. The mus-
ic performing at either end of the line could
be clearly heard at the opposite end, different
voices and different instruments being dis-
tinguishable. The transmssion of the sound
makes the music still more melodious and con-
veys to the mind the idea of music heard in
dreams, sweet and low. We were permitted
to ask one question and the answer was as dis-
tinct as though the person had stood by our
side. The Telephone will, no doubt be made a
means of saving time and labor; people can
transact business without leaving home, and
carry on conversations with persons in other
places without the trouble of writing letters or
sending telegrams even. This means of con-
veying sound is but in its first stages of de-
velopment, and no doubt will be made practi-
cable in many ways and for many purposes
not now apparent.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		141


R. S. REPORT.

	SNOWVILLE, BOX ELDER CO., Jan. 15, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Snowville is a new settlement, situated in a
large and beautiful valley on the north of the
Great Salt Lake with good facilities for a large
town.
	On Jan. 10, our respected Bishop organized a
Relief Society, when the following officers
were unanimously elected; Mrs. Mary Good-
liffe, President; Ellen Robbins and Mary Ann
Osterhout, Counselors; Esther Goodliffe, Secre-
tary and Mary Patter, Treasurer. Six Teach-
ers were also appointed. The meeting was re-
plete with good instructions and a good spirit
was manifested. The brethren present gave
us good teachings, and the sisters expressed a
thankfulness that we conld now meet together
in the capacity of a Relief Society, to enjoy
the blessings to be derived therefrom, as we,
one and all, know it is our duty to do all we
can in the great cause of truth.
	Ever wishing the success of the EXPONENT
and your happiness, we remain your sisters.
			ESTHER GOODLIFFE, Sec'y.


[Column 1]
DISCOURSE
BY
PRESIDENT BRIGHAM YOUNG
DELIVERED
In the Tabernacle, Ogden.

[CONCLUDED.]

	If you have not the mulberry trees—I have
proffered for years to give the trees and if you
want a thousand you are welcome to them, or
a million, I am ready to give them to you.
Some twenty years ago I sent for mulberry
seed. I have raised thonsands and tens of
thousands of trees, and they are in this Terri-
tory. I have a large cocoonery that I built
twelve or fourteen years ago. I have given the
use of that—a building about 20 x 110 feet, and
I have given the use of the mulberry trees, and
given the people all the trees they want. They
are a beautiful shade tree and the fruit is good.

[Column 2]
A great many people are fond of the fruit; it
is healthy for children.
	When you feel disposed to make a little
money, go into the raising of silk, which is one
of the easiest branches of business that was
ever followed. There is no other work the
women can do that will yield the same amount
of profit. This is a matter that I wish you to
hearken to. Will you bless yourselves, and do
good to yourselves? We have plenty of weav-
ers who can take the silk and know how to
manufacture it: and they will give you all you
could reasonably ask for it.
	Now let the beauty of your adorning be the
work of your own hands. Will you not, Pres-
idents, ask your Societies to enter into this
agreement, and go to work and make what
you want to wear. Then we will appeal to the
brethren and say, "come, let us wear the head-
dresses that our wives and our sisters can
make; and it would be very healthy for the
men to wear straw hats winter and summer.
We would not see so many bald heads as we
now see around here. Straw hats are perfectly
healthy to wear. I have worn them through
winter; and the only objection I have to them
is that they are a little too tight and close.
Let the sisters go to work and make these
things.
	Now, sisters, I plead with you to stop these
fashions. They are nonsense. Bro. Carring-
ton has given you a fine detail of them. They
are miserable looking. I dare not tell you
how they look to me, and how the vanity looks
that is in the minds of the people. How long
is it since my family said to me of hoops,
"They are so nice and comely; how would we
look were we to take these hoops off? why we
would look like the town pump. Would you
not be ashamed of us?" I am ashamed. I am
ashamed to see the tight clothes—to see the
shape of the ladies. How long is it since the
sleeves were so loose, that when you go into
into a store and the gentleman says, "are you
not going to buy a pair of sleeves?" "O, if I
buy a pair of sleeves I shall have to have a
new dress."  "O, I will give you a dress."
Eighteen yards in the sleeves, and three yards
in the dress! These foolish fashions what good
do they do? I have asked my sisters what
they would think if a lady who lives in heaven
should pay them a visit. Would she come
with these large sleeves on—a mu[-]ton leg
sleeve, with her dress pulled right out in front
of her? Now, it is pulled out here. It is very
unwise. It is nonsense and uncomely. It is
the best looking of anything in the world
when Brother Carrington sees his wife in her
new calico dress. "You look just as you did
when I courted you." Now there is another
fashion. You see a girl with her hair clipped
off in front of her head; she looks as though
she had just come out of a lunatic asylum.
The hair is for an ornament. You can love a
woman with a comely dress on of her own
make, just as well as though she had on a dress
that cost five thousand pounds.
	We do not seem to realize that we have to
give an account of the days we spend in folly,
and that we will be found wanting if we spend
our time foolishly. When you come to the
wheat and the fine flour, to the gold and the
silver and the prescious stones, the Lord owns
them. But what have we? Our time. Spend
it as you will. Time is given to you; and when
this is spent to the best possible advantage for
promoting truth upon the earth, it is placed to
your account, and blessed are you; but when
we spend our time in idleness and folly, it will
be placed against us. Here is the difference.
	Now, Sisters, take hold; do this that we ask
you to do. It is for your own benefit, and


health, and life, and comfort of the people,
and the building up of Zion. And let us go
to, and establish the Zion of God upon the
earth, that we may be prepared to enjoy it,
which I most earnestly pray for every day, in
the name of Jesus. Amen.


OBITUARIES.

	It becomes our painful duty to record the
death of another of our beloved sisters, Isa-
bella Layton, wife of Christopher Layton,
Kaysville, and daughter of Richard and Isa-
bella Golightly of Salt Lake City, both now
deceased. She departed this life on Saturday,
December 15, 1877. She died as she had lived
a true Latter-day Saint, full of faith in the
holy priesthood and in the order of Celestial
Marriage which she embraced in her youth, in
the year 1854, at the age of eighteen. Al-
though her path has not always been strewn
with flowers, she has like a rock in the ocean
been immovable. True to her Father in heav-
en, true to her children, true to her husband,
and to her religion, to her last moments. She
has left seven children, four sons and three
daughters. Lightly as the order of Plural
Marriage may be treated by some, it gives to
those who comprehend it joy and consolation
to see one who has borne the heat and burden
of the day for twenty-three years, pass away
so beautifully as our sister did. Her sufferings
were great for a long time. She was afflicted
with dropsy of which she died; yet her faith in
the Gospel never faltered. Many times during
her affliction she would say, how thankful she
was that her lot was cast among the Saints of
God; and that she had raised her children
where they could be cared for, and taught the


principles of our holy religion. Sisters, such
examples should encourage us to press on to
the end of our journey, for be assured that
there is eternal life for us, if we will live faith-
ful and true to our covenants. Deceased was
a Teacher in the Relief Society of Kaysville
which office she filled cheerfully as long as she
was able. She was kind and true to all her
many friends and acquaintances. She was
born in Newcastle upon Tyne, Northumber-
landshire, England, Sept. 6th. 1836, and emi-
grated to Utah in 1853. May it be our happy
lot to endure to the end as our sister has done,
is the prayer of your sister in the Gospel,
						SARAH LAYTON.
	Kaysville, Feb. 7th, 1878.

	DIED AT North Ogden, Weber Co., Utah, on
Sunday, Jan. 27th, 1877, of child-bed fever,
Eva Elizabeth Clark, beloved wife of Charles
Clark, and daughter of Abraham and Mary
Chadwick, aged twenty-one years, three
months and four days. Deceased has ever
been a faithful member of the church, and
held the office of president of the Young La-
dies' Retrenchment Society in North Ogden
from its organization, March, 24th, 1875, until
within a recent date of her death. Her con-
duct was always of that exemplary character
which denotes the true Christian; she leaves a
husband and young babe to mourn her loss,
who are sympathized with by a large circle of
friends.
		 	MARION WHEELOCK, Pres. R. S.
	Eeb., 9th, 1878.


142		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			A TRUE LADY.

	While passing along down the stream of
time and going through mixed multitudes
of people among the Latter-day saints as
well as the rest of the human family; we
are shocked many times at the appearance
of some ladies (so considered) who stand in
responsible places; we stop and reflect and
ask ourselves the question, what does it
take to constitute a true lady? Beauty and
style are not the surest passports to re-
spectability. I think some of the noblest
specimens of womanhood that the world
has ever seen, have presented the plainest,
and most unprepossessing appearance. A
woman's worth is to be estimated by the
real goodness of her heart, the greatness of
her soul, and the purity and sweetness of
her character; and a woman with a kindly
disposition and a well-balanced mind and
temper is lovely and attractive; be her face
ever so plain, and her figure ever so homely,
she makes the best of wives and the truest
of mothers. She has a higher purpose in
living than the beautiful yet vain, and su-
percilious woman, who has no higher am-
bition than to flaunt her finery on the
streets, or to gratify her inordinate vanity
by extracting flattery and praise, from a so-
ciety whose compliments are as hollow as
they are insincere. I consider a true lady
must be genteel in her manner, at the same
time courteous and obliging, and remember
that true politeness consists in treating oth-
ers just as you wish to be treated yourself.
Those who always act upon this principle,
will prove pleasant companions, will always
have pleasing and agreeable ways; nature
is always graceful, while affectation with
all the art she may employ, can never pro-
duce anything half so charming. The idea
should be ever present to the mind of the
young lady, it is the duty of every woman
to cultivate those attractive graces so beau-
tiful in the sex; but the charm is lost un-
less they strive to awaken all the energies
of the mind, and put them to their proper
use; in this way a lady may do a vast
amount of good, which will render greater
attractions than any personal charm, and
transmit to her name greater honor than if
she inherited her tens of thousands yearly.
	The glitter of wealth and fashion are as
but a flare of light that attracts the eye as
it passes by, while the influence of a noble-
hearted woman will extend far beyond her
earthly life, living in the hearts of those
who were conscious of her good works.
	When friends call upon a true lady she
will treat them with great politeness, what-
ever the existing circumstances may be
she will make them welcome. How cheer-
ing it is to a travaler weary and far from
home to receive cordial hospitality at the
house of a friend. The true lady will ex-
tend kindness to her family circle, where
her influence will effect most beneficially
in giving tone to the home, and the dar-
ling little ones will partake of it; her pleas-
ant ways will shine "like a light that is set
upon a table that giveth light to all that are
in the room. The many great and noble
qualities that belong to a true lady who is a
firm believer in the Gospel, and also in the
good results therefrom are too numerous to
mention. When I behold a lady possessing
these principles so noble to me, she looks

[Column 2]
more like an angel than mortal. When I
was in the temple, I looked and wondered
if the angels looked even more charming
than those ladies who officiate there, they
are so genteel, so courteous and kind in
their manner and appearance, so motherly
and sisterly to all.			D. F. COX.
	BEAVER, Jan. 28, 1878.


[Column 1]
PEN PICTURES.

BY DORA DARMOORE.

No. 9.

MADAME KUT-A-DASHE.

	It was at the opera I saw her. The first
act of "Don Giovanni" was nearly through
when she came sweeping in, with that ob-
sequious usher (who is always so polite to
well dressed people who have reserved
seats, and so curt to those unfortunates who
have none) clearing the way before her to
her place in the dress-circle, and her equally
obsequious husband bringing up the rear
and bearing her ladyship's opera-cloak, bou-
quet, fan and lorgnette.
	In reaching her seat she of course has to
pass before several scores of people who
have come to the opera to hear the music,
and whom

MADAME KUT-A-DASHE'S ENTREE

caused to lose one of Signorina Flute voice's
most charming "arias," and numberless
bright eyes shot forth sparks of indignation
as their owners' handsome toilettes were
ruthlessly trodden upon while Madame
Kut-a-dashe and her escorts crowded past,
quite oblivious of the trouble and annoy-
ance she was causing. Indeed, she had all
the airs of

"One who treads alone
Some banquet-hall deserted,"

and was evidently disgusted to find that
there were others in the immediate vicin-
ity. At last she reached her seat, which
was in the most conspicuous portion of the 
house, and with the air of a hen settling
herself upon her first nest-full of eggs—
ruffling her feathers, and spreading her
wings over as much space as possible—did
Madame Kut-a-dashe spread her draperies
about her, apparently quite unconscious of
the hundred opera-glasses that were in-
stantly leveled at her, mostly, I am con-
strained to confess, in the hands of ladies
anxious to see what she wore. With the
greatest nonchalance she proceeded to draw
on

HER JEWELED HANDS

the delicate lavender kids which her obe-
dient husband at a nod handed her. This
office is usually performed in the dressing-
room before entering the opera, but she
doubtless reasoned that in that case the
public would lose the opportunity of be-
holding the beauty of her bracelets, which
could be displayed to great advantage while
her husband was engaged in buttoning the
gloves over the white wrists.
	As a faithful delineator I must state that
Madame Kut-a-dashe's beauty lies entirely
in her wardrobe. Her face is pale and her
eyes sunken from the effects of late hours,
dissipation, rich viands and fashionable
dressing. Her hair is red, and was, of
course, arranged in the latest and, to such
a face as hers, most unbecoming style. On
the topmost point of the pyramid of hair
was perched an affair of velvet and flowers,
which Madame De Fuss, the milliner, calls
a bonnet, but which much more resembles
the mortars used for compounding drugs in

[Column 2]
apothecary shops. Near the northwest cor-
ner of her mouth, which by the way was
not particularly small, nor bore the least
resemblance to the traditional Cupid's bow,
was a small, black, oblong patch, placed
there heaven knows for what purpose, un-
less it was to call attention to

THE CONTEMPTUOUS SNEER

that perpetually sat upon her lips, as if she
were mentally contrasting her own enviable
position, wealth and faultless toilet with
that of the "vulgar herd" who surrounded
her.
	And yet she was not happy. One could
see that in every uneasy movement of her
restless eyes, in the irritable shrugging of
her velvet covered shoulders; in the frown
that made her sallow face still more unat-
tractive. What was needed to add to her
contentment? The idols that she wor-
shipped were at her service. The silken
dress heavily embossed with velvet, the
massive chain with its gem-studded locket;
the gleaming diamonds in her ears, on her
breast and fingers; the costly furs; these
were her idols, at whose shrine she daily
and hourly worshipped, and they were all
hers. Where, then, is

THE SECRET OF HER DISCONTENT!

	It is this: she has only the external em-
blems of happiness. Of the only true con-
tentment that makes a life in the humble
cottage, with garments of the plainest ma-
terial, peaceful and happy, she was utterly
ignorant. Her only idea of happiness is
embodied in fine houses, costly equipages,
elegant furniture and magnificent toilets,
whose splendor shall throw every assem-
blage which she enters into a fever ef ex-
citement, astonishment and envy. As she
is not always successful in producing the
required amount of the latter article, it
follows that she is often unhappy.
	There are, I am sorry to say, many Mad-
ame Kut-a-dashes in the world, and their
pernicious example has a grievous influence
on the minds and hearts of numbers of the 
"coming women." For the sake of the
peace and contentment, as well as the truth,
modesty, and good sense of these "future
mothers of the race," I pray that their
tribe may "grow smaller by degr[-]es and
beautifully less," until not one specimen is
left as a libel on true womanhood.—Mis-
sion Mirror.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

			CLIFTON, Feb. 5, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	It is with a feeling of thankfulness to
God for the blessings and privileges we
enjoy I write, feeling I must give expres-
sion, through your valuable paper, to the
sentiments of my heart. I realize my in-
ability to write my thoughts properly, ex-
cept by the Spirit of God. I do feel thank-
ful for the good instruction the EXPONENT
contains, and grateful to God for the intelli-
gent women in our midst, who are not
afraid to stand up for woman's rights, and
speak in her defense. I feel interested in
woman having all the rights that belong to
her. Why should she not have the privi-
lege to expand the intelligence that God
has given her, in something more than
household duties and dress? Why should
she not have the privilege of voting for the
officers that govern her, as well as her sons
and husband? I feel to say God bless the
noble women who are laboring for the rights
of woman! Why did God give her intel-
lect equal to man's, if she must not use it


for the welfare of her fellow-beings, and to
promote good in a broader sphere than
housewifery affords?  I think she ought to
have all the rights and blessings she is ca-
pacitated to enjoy and honor, that she may
be better qualified to bring up her sons to
make wise and useful men in the land; and
to teach her daughtersaright that they may
be the wise mothers of a future generation,
that we may keep increasing in intelligence,
until we become a noble people, zealous of
good works, and full of wisdom and under-
standing: this is just what the Lord wants
His people to be.  Ever praying for the
prosperity of all good works, and all who
labor in building up Zion.	ELIZA.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


DEATH IN THE DISHCLOTH.

	A lady says, in the "Rural World," that
during the sickly months, when some of
you are sure to be down with the typhoid
fever, when neighbors are neglecting their
own work to nurse you, when doctors are
hunting in cellars and old drains for the
cause, let me whisper in your ear, Look to
your dishcloths.  If they be black and stiff,
and smell like a "bone-yard," it is enough
—throw them in the fire, and from hence-
forth and forever wash your dishes with
cloths that are white, cloths that you can
see through, and see if you ever have the
disease again.  There are sometimes other
causes, but I have smelled a whole houseful
of typhoid fever in one "dishrag." I had
some neighbors once—clever, good sort of
folk; one Fall four of them were sick at one
time with typhoid fever.  The doctors or-
dered the vinegar barrels whitewashed,
and threw about forty cents' worth of car-
bolic acid in the swill-pail, and departed.
I went into the kitchen to make gruel; I
needed a dishcloth and looked about and
found several, and such "rags!"  I burned
them all, and called the daughter of the
house to get me a dishcloth.  She looked
round on the tables.  "Why," said she,
"there was about a dozen here this morn-
ing;" and she looked in the woodbox and
on the mantelpiece, and felt in the dark
corner of the cupboard.  "Well," I said, "I
saw some old, black, rotten rags lying
round and I burned them, for there is death
in such dishcloths as those, and you must
never use such again."  I "took turns,"
nursing that family four weeks, and I be-
lieve those dirty dishcloths were the cause
of all that hard work.  Therefore I say to
every housekeeper, keep your dishcloths
clean.  You may wear your dresses with-
out ironing, your sunbonnets without elas-
tics—but you must keep your dishcloths
clean.  You may only comb your hair on
Sundays, you need not wear a collar unless
you go from home—but you must wash
your dishcloth.  You may only sweep the
floor "when the sign gets right;" the win-
dows don't need washing, you can look out
at the door; that spider's web on the front
porch don't hurt anything—but, as you love
your lives, wash out your dishcloth.  Let
the foxtail get ripe in the garden (the seed
is a foot deep any way), let the holes in the
heels of your husband's foot rags go un-
darned, let the sage go ungathered, let the
children's shoes go two Sundays without
blacking, let two hens sit four weeks on
one wooden egg—but do wash your dish-
cloths.  Eat without a table-cloth, wash
your faces and let them dry, do without a
curtain for your windows, and cake for your
tea, but, for heaven's sake, keep your dish-
cloths clean.


[Column 1]
	THE BIBLE AND ITS TEACHINGS.
	SERMON BY REV. DR. A. HUEB[-]CH AT
	THE TEMPLE AHAWATH CHESED.

	The Rev. Dr. Huebsch preached a ser-
mon on the above subject at the Jewish tem-
ple Ahawatch Chesed, Lexington avenue,
not long since. His text was taken from
Genesis, ii.—"This is the book of the gen-
erations of Adam. On the day that God
created man, in the likeness of God made
He him."
	The great Roman orator, said the Doctor,
has pronounced it that books had their fate.
Some are still born, some live for a short
period and then expire; the very few that
survive in the length of time become slaves;
chained to the shelves of the antiquarian
they cease to be fields on which life reaps
its harvest of wisdom and change into the
secluded hunting ground, where the liter-
ary chaser hunts after the rare game of an-
tique oddities. One book makes an excep-
tion. Its glorious destiny was sealed by
its first appearance and in spite of rolling
time and its destructive power this book,
with never abating strength, serves its des-
tination. If there be no other proof of the
divine origin of the Bible its perpetual
youth, its never slacking use and usefulness,
speak sufficiently of its superiority over all
human wisdom. It is the book for all times
and all men; it teaches the king justice and
humility, and infuses trust and dignity in-
to the beggar; it shows to the learned the
boundaries where human wisdom ends, and
brings the dawning spirit of the child to
the gates where human wisdom begins; it
advises the joyful to enhance and purify
his joys, and informs mercifully the aching
and breaking heart how to alleviate sorrow
and mourning. It raises the finger of warn-
ing toward the youth not to trust in the
abundance of strength, and at the same
time lends the fortifying staff to the weary
wanderer for his last steps through the
dark valley of death. It speaks to nations
and to individuals, to all humanity and to
every single member of human kind. It
promulgates the highest principles of poli-
tics, and imparts the modest rules for the
humble household; it embraces man in all
dimensions, earth in all climates, time in all
changes, and eternity in all its glory. One
would think that to cover such an immense
range of instruction a system must be ap-
plied which is complicated and and difficult
to be comprehended. It is not so. The
whole book is based on one cardinal point,
from which all the streams of light break
forth with such abundance, strength and
intensity as to penetrate into all circles and
classes of humanity's life.
	The fountainhead of all human morality
is concentrated in the one truth: on the day
that God created man "in the likeness of
God made he him!" From this relation of
man to God issues the infallible arrange-
ment of all relations between man and
man; moral affinity and moral associations
—this is the Alpha and Omega of the Scrip-
tural system. The faultiness of human life
is the sad consequence of swerving from
this system.
	The Doctor reviewed the various branch-
es of human activity, and how they are and
how they ought to be. He spoke about the
amount of genius, physical strength and
the money which is wasted in Europe at 
this moment to destroy man and his pros-
perity. If the same means were applied to
further the welfare of mankind, would we
not progress further in one decade than we
do in centuries? The great book proclaims
love—love founded on moral aff[-]inity—and,

[Column 2]
therefore, it will always remain sound and
vigorous in its teaching; and the regenera-
tion of humankind will take place, when
the Divine Book will be the respected and
obeyed moral adviser of all the children of
Adam.


R. S. REPORT.

	KANAB, Jan. 25, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT.
	In Nov. 1873 the sisters of Kanab Ward
were called together by Bishop Levi Stu-
art, and a Relief Society was organized
with Sister Sarah Crosby as President.  Ow-
ing to the ill-health and many cares of the
President and some of the other officers
they gave in their resignations, which were
accepted.  Dec. 15, 1877, the sisters of the
ward met under the direction of President
James L. Bunting, and Bishop W. D. John-
son, jun., and were reorganized with Mrs.
Harriet Bunting as president, Mrs. Arte-
macy Stewart, and Mrs. Lucy A. Johnson
as counselors; Mrs. M. Elizabeth Little Sec-
retary, Miss Lovinia Johnson, Assistant
Secretary, and Mrs. Elizabeth Solomon,
Treasurer. 
	Bishop W. D. Johnson jun., at the same
meeting organized a Young Ladies Mutual
Improvement Association, with Mrs. So-
phia T. Nuttal, President, Mrs. Sarah L.
Mariger and Miss Elizabeth A. Nuttal'as
Counselors, Miss M. M. Farnsworth Secre-
tary, and Miss E. Cram as Treasurer.
	The brethren gave excellent counsel con-
cerning the duties and priviliges of Relief
Societies, and exhorted the sisters to seek
after the spirit of the Lord, that they may
be fitted for the great work before them.
	With the help of the Lord we intend to
make the future a success.  The EXPONENT
is taken by several of the sisters, and is
read with interest.  In future we will try 
to be known among the sisters of Zion in
that excellent paper.
	The people of Kanab feel that the passing
year will be to them a new era.  We hope
the time is not distant when the saying of
the late President Brigham Young, will be
realized, “that Kanab shall be a prosperous
and beautiful city.”
	M. ELIZABETH LITTLE, Sec’y.


WHY SOME PEOPLE ARE POOR.

	Silver spoons are used to scrape kettles.
	Coffee, tea, pepper and spice, are left to
stand open and lose their strength.
	Potatoes in the cellar grow, and sprouts
are not removed until the potatoes become
worthless.
	Brooms are never hung up and are soon 
spoiled.
	Nice handled knives are thown into hot
water.
	The flour is sifted in a wasteful manner,
and the bread-pan is left with the dough
sticking to it.
	Clothes are left on the line to whip to
pieces in the wind.
	Tubs and barrels are left in the sun to
dry and fall apart.
	Dried fruits are not taken care of in sea-
son and become wormy.
	Rags, strings, and paper are thrown into 
the fire.
	Pork spoils for want of salt, and beef be-
cause the brine wants scalding.
	Bits of meat, vegetables, bread, and cold
puddings are thrown away, when they 
might be warmed, steamed, and served as
good as new.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE HEARING IN WASHINGTON.

	EDITORS JOURNAL.—Not having as yet
seen in your paper any account of the hear-
ing granted by the United States Senate
Committee on Privileges and Elections, to
the petitioners for a Sixteenth Amendment,
and as this was really the most important
feature of the recent effort in Washington,
I venture to send you some account of it.
	A striking characteristic of the late con-
vention was the large number of delegates
present from all parts of the country, most
of them women of ability and all of the
highest respectability. Representing, as
they did, such large sections of the Union,
the Committe, at considerable inconven-
ience to its members, granted two succes-
sive hearings.
	The Committee itself consisted of Sena-
tors Wadleigh, of New Hampshire, Mitch-
ell, of Oregon, Cameron, of Wisconsin,
McMillan, of Minnesota, Hoar, of Massa-
chusetts, Salisbury, of Delaware, and Hill,
of Georgia.
	On Friday, January 10, at 10.30 A. M,
they assembled in their committee room,
which was crowded with advocates of Wo-
man Suffrage.  Mrs. Clemence S. Lozier,
M. D., of New York, made the opening
address. She was followed by Miss Julia
Smith, of Connecticut, Mrs. Elizabeth Cady
Stanton, of New Jersey, Mrs. Matilda Jos-
lyn Gage, of New New York, Mrs. Mary A.
Stewart of Delaware, Mrs. Elizabeth Boyn-
ton Harbert, of Illinois, and Mrs. Priscilla
R. Lawrence, of Massachusetts.
	As a large number of delegates still re-
mained to be heard, by unanimous vote of
the Committee, a second hearing was grant-
ed on the following day, and in order to ac-
commodate the crowd that was anxious to
be present, this was held in the reception-room of the Senate, which was thronged to
overflowing.
	On this second day, Saturday, January
11, addresses were made by Rev. Olympia
Brown, of Connecticut, Mrs. Mary Powers
Filley, of New Hampshire, Dr. Mary
Thompson, of Oregon, Mrs. Lillie Deve-
reaux Blake, of New York, Mrs. Sara A.
Spencer, of the District of Columbia, and
Mrs. Isabella Beecher Hooker, of Connec-
ticut.
	At the conclusion of the hearing, Senator
Hoar presented a resolution declaring that
the arguments had been presented in a very
able and dignified manner; and the compli-
ment was well deserved, as each woman
had urged some local matter showing how
the women of her State suffered for the
want of the ballot, or presented one of the
great national arguments in its favor.
	A short hand reporter was employed by
the committee to take down all the speeches,
and the committee has since decided that
fifty thousand copies shall be printed, at
the expense of the Senate, a compliment
quite unprecedented.	L. D. B.
	New York.	—Woman's Journal.


AURELIA M. GILBERT, M. D., a graduate
of the Boston University medical depart-
ment, is located in Louisville, Ky., and is
establishing a practice there.
